text: 22). Due to high costs, risks, and lengthy construction pro- cesses, nuclear energy has been in decline for several years, yet it could become increasingly relevant with the development of new technolo- gies, such as small modular reactors (IAE, 2023). At COP28 in Dubai, 22 countries signed a pledge to triple nuclear energy capacity by 2050, recognizing the role of nuclear power in keeping the 1.5

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: izing the role of nuclear power in keeping the 1.5 ◦C goal alive (NEA, 2023). The use of nuclear power is, however, disputed due to the risk of accidents and unresolved waste management issues (Gupta et al., 2019; Wang and Kim, 2018). Interestingly though, attitudes toward nuclear power appear to be determined more by political and ideolog- ical factors, values, and worldviews than by risk percept

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ctors, values, and worldviews than by risk perceptions and eco- nomic considerations (Whitfield et al., 2009). Overall, the support for further nuclear investments in the European Union is fairly weak (Eurobarometer, 2022). To successfully navigate a pathway toward a low-carbon and sus- tainable future, it is essential to understand how different social and value-based dimensions influence energy

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ocial and value-based dimensions influence energy policies. This article aims to contribute to the literature by exploring factors that determine energy opinions, focusing on the polarization of wind and nuclear preferences in Sweden. Sweden is an interesting case study, as it is a country with a high level of both wind and nuclear energy in its energy mix, yet one where energy policies are marked

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ergy mix, yet one where energy policies are marked by deep political tensions and polar- ization. The study draws conclusions from a large-scale survey con- ducted in Sweden during 2023, including over 5200 respondents, who were randomly selected and representative of the wider Swedish population. The results show that low-carbon energy investments in Sweden are likely to encounter resistance due

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: in Sweden are likely to encounter resistance due to a sizable antagonistic minority who are strongly opposed to either wind or nuclear energy. Interestingly, among those with traditional, nationalistic, and authoritarian values and right leaning political ideology, the enthusiasm for nuclear energy seems to reduce the closer a new nuclear power plant would be to their own residences. The study hi

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ant would be to their own residences. The study highlights the importance of recognizing the sociopsychological dimensions within political frameworks aiming for a transition toward a low-carbon energy system. 2. Overview of relevant literature Attitudes to wind and nuclear power are divisive in many countries. Both of these energy technologies generate different social emotions in different socie

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: erate different social emotions in different societal groups, often related to beliefs about benefits and risks associated with the respective technology. Factors influencing attitudes to different energy options tend to be contextual and they vary across different countries (Arndt, 2023; Wang and Kim, 2018; Nguyen and Yim, 2018). Opinions about nuclear energy are particularly influenced by normat

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: clear energy are particularly influenced by normative aspects related to the risk of accidents, uranium extraction, the treatment of radioactive waste, and its association with the military industry (Siegrist and Visschers, 2013; Keller et al., 2012; Groh and Mo¨llendorff, 2020; Siegrist et al., 2014). Support for nuclear energy has clearly been affected by the three major accidents worldwide—Thre

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: fected by the three major accidents worldwide—Three Mile Island, Chernobyl, and Fukushima—after which public attitudes turned more negative (Richard Eiser et al., 1990; Visschers and Siegrist, 2013). Nuclear power can, on the other hand, also be associated with energy security and economic prosperity (Slovic et al., 2000; Arndt, 2023), and social acceptance can, thus, be described as a trade-off b

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: cceptance can, thus, be described as a trade-off be- tween risks and potential economic benefits (Finucane et al., 2000; Slovic et al., 2004). People’s perceptions of risks and benefits differ, however, depending on individual-level factors. Aspects that have been demonstrated to in- fluence opinions are political ideology, gender, income, and education (Costa-Font et al., 2008; Sundstro¨m and McC

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ation (Costa-Font et al., 2008; Sundstro¨m and McCright, 2016; Gupta et al., 2019; Spence et al., 2010; Hazboun and Boudet, 2020), as well as institutional and country-level factors, such as the national energy mix and political aspects (Wang and Kim, 2018; Bohdanowicz et al., 2023). 2 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 Renewable energy is not associated with similar risk perceptions, and i

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ot associated with similar risk perceptions, and in many countries, wind energy is among the most popular energy options (Arndt, 2023). Attitudes expressed at nation-level tend to diverge, however, from the views of the communities living in the proximity of wind power investment (Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017; Dug- stad et al., 2020). Bell et al. (2005)expressed accordingly that a “social gap” tends t

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: )expressed accordingly that a “social gap” tends to arise between the high level of public support at the na- tional level, and the low level of successful implementation of wind energy projects. The distance of wind energy sitings from peoples’ residence, the so- called proximity effect, has shown to have a strong impact on opinions (Carley et al., 2020; Karasmanaki and Tsantopoulos, 2021). Early

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: , 2020; Karasmanaki and Tsantopoulos, 2021). Early studies on social acceptance of wind power focused accordingly on the proximity effect or so-called NIMBY-ism (not-in-my-back-yard) (Batel et al., 2013; Fast, 2013). However, there is criticism of the “NIMBY-- term”, which is sometimes used as a method to discredit opponents (Horst, 2007). While noise and visual intrusion of wind farms have shown

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ise and visual intrusion of wind farms have shown to influence public attitudes (Carley et al., 2020; Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017), opposing responses are often based on more complex aspects (Dermont et al., 2017; Batel, 2020). Devine-Wright (2009), Horst (2007) and Dugstad et al. (2023) show that socio-cultural attachment to the place of living is of relevance, and that opposition can be seen as “pl

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: relevance, and that opposition can be seen as “place-protective action”. Public acceptance of wind power is often influenced by the perceived environmental impacts of the wind farm location, and to what extent biodiversity in landscapes are affected (Dugstad et al., 2020). Biodiversity concerns differ, however, between different communities, and in Sweden, they are of particular relevance for Sam

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: n Sweden, they are of particular relevance for Sami communities (Ramasar et al., 2022; Ek and Matti, 2015). Hence, careful analysis of the spatial allocation of wind farms is important when addressing inherent trade-offs between local dis- amenities, biodiversity conservation, and climate change considerations (Kati et al., 2021; Grimsrud et al., 2024). Geographic proximity does not have similar i

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: 024). Geographic proximity does not have similar impacts on attitudes on nuclear energy, and studies show instead that people living close to nuclear power plants are more positively inclined than those living further away (Greenberg, 2009; Uji et al., 2021; Venables et al., 2012; Cale and Kromer, 2015). However, few studies have captured attitudes in relation to proximity prior to the constructio

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: in relation to proximity prior to the construction process. It is likely that new nuclear power plant establishments in landscapes, just like new wind-power plants, may evoke negative reactions motived by sense of place or concerns about their impacts on local wild life and biodiversity (Sovacool, 2012). Recent studies indicate, moreover, that opposition to wind power is associated with notions o

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: osition to wind power is associated with notions of being neglected or misrepresented (Walker and Devine-Wright, 2008). There are also research suggesting that perceptions of unfair distribution of the benefits and burdens of wind power influence acceptance (Huijts et al., 2012; De Luca et al., 2020). If profits from wind power generation is not redistributed locally, but transferred to the nation

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: distributed locally, but transferred to the national level or to business corporations, individuals tend to see the construction of wind power as a kind of exploitation of the local landscape (Lindvall, 2023; Liebe et al., 2017; Knauf, 2022). The decision-making process and the ability of affected individuals to participate in it, also impact acceptance of wind power (Walker and Baxter, 2017; Bren

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: tance of wind power (Walker and Baxter, 2017; Brennan and Rensburg, 2016; Le Maitre et al., 2023; Vuichard et al., 2022). Individuals who distrust public institutions, or who perceive the decision-making processes as unfair, non-transparent, or illegitimate, are also more likely to oppose wind farms (Gross, 2007; Liebe et al., 2017). Perceptions of procedural and distributional justice have also b

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: procedural and distributional justice have also been shown to influence public attitudes to nuclear energy (Visschers and Siegrist, 2012; Besley, 2012), although the research on this matter is limited. Attitudes to different energy options are, however, not only deter- mined by aspects related to fairness and risks, but also by other types of personal norms, values, and ideological orientations.

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: onal norms, values, and ideological orientations. A particularly strong determinant for both wind and nuclear energy attitudes is envi- ronmental concern. Individuals who prioritize environmental protection 2 D. Lindvall et al. over economic aspects, are found to be more skeptical about nuclear energy, while those who perceive nuclear energy as economically beneficial are more prone to downplay en

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: nomically beneficial are more prone to downplay environmental hazards (Pampel, 2011; Badora et al., 2021; Besley and Oh, 2014; Corner et al., 2011). Individuals with pro-environmental attitudes tend to, likewise, be more positive to wind energy (Azarova et al., 2019; Knauf, 2022), although concerns of the local environmental impacts may also motivate resis- tance (Dugstad et al., 2020). Some studi

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: te resis- tance (Dugstad et al., 2020). Some studies indicate that climate change concern has become less relevant for nuclear power acceptance (Bohdanowicz et al., 2023), yet other studies have not found evidence of this trend (Sonnberger et al., 2021; Arndt, 2023). Several studies suggest, moreover, that attitudes toward nuclear energy are associated with political ideology. Individuals who defi

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ated with political ideology. Individuals who define themselves politically to the left tend to be more positive to wind power (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021), while the opposite is true for nuclear energy (Whitfield et al., 2009; Costa-Font et al., 2008; Pampel, 2011; Clulow et al., 2021). Worldviews are also relevant, and individuals who express individualistic or hierarchical

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: duals who express individualistic or hierarchical values tend to be more supportive of nuclear energy (Van De Graaff, 2016; Whitfield et al., 2009), while wind power is preferred by individuals who express egalitarian and ecological values (Bidwell, 2013). As a result, attitudes to wind and nuclear energy are prone to po- litical polarization (Perlaviciute and Steg, 2015; Roddis et al., 2019). The

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: aviciute and Steg, 2015; Roddis et al., 2019). The association between ideology and values, and public attitudes to different energy technologies, can therefore be influenced by political narratives or political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019), and media messaging and ˙ political debates (Ho and Kristiansen, 2019; Zuk, 2023; Kojo et al., 2020). Some studies have also shown that attitudes can be exploit

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: dies have also shown that attitudes can be exploited by economic agents or interest groups advocating for or against the usage of renewable energy (Johnstone et al., 2017). 3. Energy policies in Sweden 3.1. The polarised debate on nuclear energy Sweden is a country where energy policies have been fraught with difficult political disputes for over five decades. In the 1970s, Sweden developed a nucl

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ive decades. In the 1970s, Sweden developed a nuclear energy program, commissioning 12 nuclear re- actors. These reactors were constructed between 1972 and 1980, mak- ing Sweden the country with the highest percentage of nuclear power usage per capita in the world (Kåberger, 2002). The construction of nuclear power plants was partly motivated by the needs and demands of the industry, but it was al

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e needs and demands of the industry, but it was also a response to the 1973 oil price shock and the decision to halt the expansion of hydropower to protect the few remaining unexploited rivers in Sweden (Vedung, 2002). Initially, there was strong interparty consensus for the nuclear energy program; how- ever, the expansion became an increasingly contentious political issue, partly spurred by the a

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ntentious political issue, partly spurred by the anti-nuclear stance taken by the Centre Party (Vedung, 1979). From 1976, nuclear energy emerged as one of the most disruptive issues in Swedish politics, causing a government crisis in 1978 and profoundly affecting the 1979 election results (Holmberg, 2022). Following the accident at Three Mile Island, anti-nuclear senti- ments grew stronger, and to

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: d, anti-nuclear senti- ments grew stronger, and to defuse the issue, a public referendum was held in 1980. A slight majority of voters supported a gradual phase-out of nuclear power in the referendum; however, the process of phasing out nuclear energy did not begin until 1999, when the first reactor was decommissioned. Since then, another five reactors have been closed down (Holmberg, 2009). At th

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: tors have been closed down (Holmberg, 2009). At the time of the referendum, opinions on nuclear energy were not initially influenced by ideological orientations but were determined by factors such as risk perception and environmental concern (Holmberg, 2018). Over time, the issue became increasingly politically polarized, and by 2021, right-leaning voters were, according to one survey, 58 percenta

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: voters were, according to one survey, 58 percentage points more likely to favor nuclear power than left-leaning voters (Holmberg, 2022). In an attempt to depoliticize energy policies, an interparty energy agreement was negotiated by all major political 3 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 parties in 2016. According to this agreement, the existing reactors were allowed to be replaced, but w

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: isting reactors were allowed to be replaced, but without any public subsidies. A long-term target of 100 percent renewable electricity production by 2040 was also adopted, and the so-called electricity certification system, benefiting renewable energy production, was prolonged and expanded (prop: 2017/18:228). In 2019, the agreement was dissolved following the departure of two conservative parties

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ollowing the departure of two conservative parties, which advocated for the continued operation of two reactors at the Ringhals power plant. The debate on nuclear energy then intensified, and in the 2022 election campaign, the pro-nuclear conservative parties ran on a proposal to construct new reactors, while the center-liberal, green, and left-leaning parties took neutral or skeptical stances (Ho

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ning parties took neutral or skeptical stances (Holmberg, 2022). The debate was also influenced by the rising popularity of the Sweden Democrats, a nationalist and right-wing populist party, which strongly advocated for nuclear power and opposed wind power development. The conservative government that took power in 2022, with collaborative support from the Sweden Democrats, declared its intention

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: from the Sweden Democrats, declared its intention to restart one of the decommissioned reactors and grant financial subsidies to any private companies willing to construct new power plants. In 2023, the govern- ment accordingly appointed an inquiry to investigate the construction of new nuclear power plants in Sweden (Regeringen, 2023a). By the end of 2023, the government also presented its Climat

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: of 2023, the government also presented its Climate Action Plan, in compliance with the Climate Act, in which it claimed that “… in Sweden nuclear power is the single most important measure to reduce our emission nationally …” (Regeringen, 2023b, p. 10). 3.2. The divided support for wind power The expansion of wind power has also become an issue characterized by growing political tension. Wind pow

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: aracterized by growing political tension. Wind power has developed rapidly in Sweden, and in between 2001 and 2021, the installed effect increased by almost ten times (Swedish Energy Agency, 2021). The expansion of wind power has been widely supported in society, yet in recent years, atti- tudes has become increasingly polarized (Jo¨nsson, 2022). Just like the debate on nuclear power, political op

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ust like the debate on nuclear power, political opinions are divided between conservative parties, which express anti-wind power rhetoric, and the more supportive center-liberal, green, and left-leaning parties (Axelsson et al., 2023). At the local level, wind power has become a particularly divisive issue. The Environmental Act and the municipal planning monopoly grant municipalities the power to

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: lanning monopoly grant municipalities the power to decline con- struction permits for wind power sites, colloquially referred to as the “municipal veto.” The number of municipal rejections has increased in recent years, and in 2021, around 80 percent of all construction appli- cations were declined (Westander, 2022). A key reason for these re- jections is the lack of formal compensation for people

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ions is the lack of formal compensation for people living near wind power sites (Lindvall, 2023). As a result, the government commissioned a public inquiry in 2021 to present proposals for a more predictable municipal siting process (SOU, 2022:53), and in 2022, another inquiry was appointed to introduce local incentives for wind power develop- ment (SOU, 2023:18). The new conservative government,

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: (SOU, 2023:18). The new conservative government, elected in 2022, expressed a negative stance on wind power during the election campaign and has withdrawn public subsidies for the connection cost of offshore wind power to the power grid. In its Climate Action Plan, the government eased its negative policy stance towards wind power, although it did not present any proposals for facilitating wind p

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: not present any proposals for facilitating wind power development or overcoming acceptance barriers (Regeringen, 2023b). 4. Research hypotheses, methodology and data material 4.1. Hypothesizing determinants for acceptance of energy investment The aim of this article is to examine potential determinants of social attitudes to wind and nuclear energy, based on the results of an exploratory survey.

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: y, based on the results of an exploratory survey. Previous studies have demonstrated that environ- mental concern is a key determinant for both wind and nuclear power, but also that political attitudes and values, such as ideology, 3 D. Lindvall et al. worldviews, and personal identity, influence attitudes to these energy options (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021; Pampel, 2011; Whit

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: haug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021; Pampel, 2011; Whitfield et al., 2009). Based on these previous findings, we as- sume that individuals with right-leaning attitudes and low climate concern, express higher support to nuclear energy, while left-leaning individuals with high climate concern, express higher support for wind power. We also assume that worldviews, that correspond with traditional, authori

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ldviews, that correspond with traditional, authoritarian, and nationalistic (TAN), or green, alternative, and libertarian (GAL) values (Hooghe et al., 2002), predict energy preferences, as well as political and institutional trust. To explore these correlations, we will test the following hypothesis. H1. Value -based orientations influence peoples’ propensity to support nuclear and wind power. In

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: propensity to support nuclear and wind power. In a situation with a high level of political polarization between different energy policies, it is likely that energy preferences are deter- mined by the attitudes toward one technology. Such effects can indicate that attitudes are determined by political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019) or politically motivated reasoning (Kahan, 2013), rather than environ

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: vated reasoning (Kahan, 2013), rather than environ- mental concerns, risk perceptions, economic benefits, energy security or other factual considerations. There could, thus, be a correlation between support for nuclear power and negative attitudes to wind power, while the correlation is reversed for support for wind power. Such a rela- tionship could indicate that polarization is a factor that can

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ld indicate that polarization is a factor that can explain at- titudes. To explore this, we will test the following hypothesis. H2. The propensity to support nuclear power is determined by nega- tive attitudes toward wind power. Another factor that has been shown to strongly influence support for wind power is the location of the sitings (Batel et al., 2013; Carley et al., 2020). The closer a wind

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: al., 2013; Carley et al., 2020). The closer a wind farm is constructed to where people live, the less likely they are to accept it. This proximity effect has not previously been shown to strongly affect people’s attitudes to nuclear power, yet it is uncertain whether people hold supportive attitudes prior to the con- struction process. It is, moreover, not clear how value-based orientations intera

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ver, not clear how value-based orientations interact with, or alleviate, the proximity effect, which could indicate how strongly attitudes are underpinned by cultural values. To explore these associations, we test the following three hypotheses. H3. The proximity of a wind power project predicts attitudes toward wind power development. H4. The proximity of a nuclear power project predicts attitude

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: imity of a nuclear power project predicts attitudes toward nuclear power development. H5. Value-based orientations mediate the proximity effect on attitudes toward nuclear and wind power. 4.2. Survey design and data The study is based on data from a large-scale public opinion survey on attitudes to climate and energy policies in Sweden. The survey was conducted within the research project Fairtran

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: was conducted within the research project Fairtrans by the survey company Skop. It included questions on both climate and energy policies, and a subset of the questions was analyzed for this article. The sample was drawn from the population register and made as a non-proportional stratified sample in three age categories and divisions between different types of municipality, in order to attain a p

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: rent types of municipality, in order to attain a proportional repre- sentation. It was sent out in postal format, with possibilities for digital responses, to 33 432 respondents aged 18 to 84 in between 25 April and 23 August, 2023. A post-stratification methodology was applied to make the sample representative of the total population. After weighting the dataset on a number of core demographics t

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ing the dataset on a number of core demographics to make it representative of the Swedish population, a total of 5280 respondents were included in the dataset. More information about the data collection and stratifica- tion methodology is provided in the supplementary material to this article. To capture both general attitudes and proximity effects, participants 4 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: , participants 4 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 were asked to respond to three statements regarding each energy tech- nology. The statements were phrased; To speed up the transformation to a fossil free society, I support further construction of wind/nuclear power in Sweden. Thereafter, the respondents were asked if they support (1) construction of wind/nuclear power in their municipali

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: truction of wind/nuclear power in their municipality and (2) construction of wind/nuclear power within 5 km from their home. Five kilometers was estimated to be considered as intrusive, realistic, and comparable between the two energy options. For each statement, the respondents were asked to indicate their level of agreement on a seven- point scale. The scale was anchored at 1 = Completely disagr

text: e. The scale was anchored at 1 = Completely disagree,1 4 = Neither/nor, and 7 =Completely agree. We thereafter designed questions on value-based orientations, aspiring to both capture positions on the left/right political spectrum and social ideals and worldviews. On the later issue, we used a scale inspired by the GAL (Green/Alternative/Libertarian) vs. TAN (Tradi- tional/Authoritarian/Nationalis

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: n) vs. TAN (Tradi- tional/Authoritarian/Nationalistic) framework (Hooghe et al., 2002). GAL/TAN values are typically assessed using a set of value judgments regarding aspects such as multiculturalism, international solidarity, gender equality on one end, and conservatism and national community on the other (Hobolt et al., 2021). GAL values were assessed on the re- sponses to the following two ques

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: essed on the re- sponses to the following two questions: “Society is enriched by open bor- ders, global cooperation and international solidarity” and “It is important to promote equality between men and women”. TAN was assessed on the following two questions: “In a secure society, immigration is low and the majority of the inhabitants share the same culture” and “In a secure society, law and order

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: e culture” and “In a secure society, law and order is maintained by a strong police force and a judiciary that can sentence cognizable punishments for criminals”. GAL is the weighted re- sponses of the two first questions, and the opposite for TAN. Two questions aimed to measure trust were created, asking re- spondents to assess if they believed politicians to most often act in the public interest

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: liticians to most often act in the public interests (political trust) and if public authorities (governmental trust) most often act competently, efficiently and in accordance with the rule of law. The same response scale as described above was used. To capture environmental concern, a subset of Schultz’s scale (Wesley schultz, 2001) was used. Respondents were asked to indicate their level of conce

title: The polarization of energy preferences - A study on social acceptance of wind and nuclear power in Sweden
text: ndents were asked to indicate their level of concern about environmental problems and their conse- quences to (1) myself, (2) all people, (3) my lifestyle, (4) people close to me, (5) animals and plants, (6) future generations, and (7) children, on a seven-point scale. The scale was anchored at 1 =Not at all concerned and 7 =Very concerned. The responses were collated into an index assessing envir

Other individual characteristics gathered in the survey regarded in- come, education, place of living (rural/urban), and gender. title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Wind energy plays an important role in the energy transition. However, many wind energy projects result in Wind energy conflicts at the local level. Mayors and local council members are key actors who can play a supportive, Social acceptance moderating, escalating, or mediating role in siting decisions about wind energy. Further, communities’ social Social nor

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: nd energy. Further, communities’ social Social norms norms encapsulate their beliefs about what a wind energy project should be like. Alongside public expectations, Incumbents these norms indicate the layers of cultural dynamics and standards of communities. Hence, this study investigates Energy transition the dynamics of local responses to wind energy projects and their outcomes. This is achieved

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ergy projects and their outcomes. This is achieved through an empirical-qualitative approach in which the experiences of four Bavarian case studies in Germany are illustrated using document analysis and in-depth interviews. The results of this study indicate that mayors play a crucial role in local responses to wind energy projects in Bavaria. Their support is necessary but not sufficient for loca

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: r support is necessary but not sufficient for local acceptance. Other stakeholders, next to project characteristics and communication, as well as external events, also have an impact on local responses over time. The paper concludes with lessons learned about communi- cation and information strategies, as the study has implications for policymakers and practitioners in relation to designing and pl

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: Availableonline4March2023 0301-4215/©2023TheAuthors. PublishedbyElsevierLtd. ThisisanopenaccessarticleundertheCCBY-NClicense(http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by- nc/4.0/). I. Karakislak and N. Schneider projects in Bavaria, Germany, focusing on the role of local politicians and social norms. German federal states define guidelines that regulate wind energy siting. Therefore, we chose only one f

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: wind energy siting. Therefore, we chose only one federal state (Bavaria) to ensure that the regulatory framework was the same. One common regulatory path for addressing acceptance-related problems is determining re- quirements for their proximity to residential areas (Masurowski et al., 2016; Watson et al., 2012). Bavaria has introduced a state regulation, the 10H rule, which defines the minimum d

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ulation, the 10H rule, which defines the minimum distance between the wind turbine and residential areas as at least ten times the total height of the wind turbine (Baugesetzbuch, 2014). With wind turbines reaching overall heights of 250 m, the 10H rule increases the challenge for mu- nicipalities to find appropriate project sites. Even though proximity has some effect on the perceptions of wind f

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: imity has some effect on the perceptions of wind farms, the intensity of this effect is shaped by the norms and values of the affected communities (van der Horst, 2007). Political power can produce and promote certain norms in social systems (Fraser, 2014), and institutional norms have the potential to regulate market dynamics (Nyborg et al., 2016). Further, this paper focuses on wind energy proje

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: . Further, this paper focuses on wind energy projects in forest areas because some of the main concerns of those who oppose wind energy projects are related to landscape protection and impacts on biodiversity, especially in forest areas (Dai et al., 2015). While deploying wind parks in forest areas is complex in terms of local acceptance in Germany, developing wind energy in such zones is necessar

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: , developing wind energy in such zones is necessary for achieving climate and renewable energy goals (FA Wind, 2021). Wind turbines in forested areas are situated almost exclusively in the southern federal states, namely, Rhineland-Palatinate, Hessen, Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg, and North Rhine-Westphalia (Bunzel et al., 2019). In Bavaria, forests account for around 37% of the land area, making it

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: account for around 37% of the land area, making it the state with the greatest forest coverage among all 16 German states (FA Wind, 2021). Analysing local actors that influence community norms can increase understanding of conflicts and dynamics related to wind energy projects (Karakislak et al., 2021). This paper aims to fill the gaps in the literature by exploring the relationship between local

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ature by exploring the relationship between local political figures and social norms and examining their effect on local responses to wind energy projects. It does so by addressing the following research questions: 1) What is the role of elected mayors in wind energy projects? 2) What influence do their opinions have on local responses? and 3) What actors and processes influence local responses ov

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: actors and processes influence local responses over time? The remainder of this article is structured as follows: Section 2 in- troduces the theoretical foundation of the paper by discussing the role of social norms, local political figures, and the local population. Section 3 introduces the methodological approach, the case study selection, the methods, and the analytical approach. Section 4 giv

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ethods, and the analytical approach. Section 4 gives a detailed over- view of the case studies. Section 5 illustrates the results of the four case studies. The paper concludes in section 6 by reviewing the main findings and suggesting implications for policymakers and practitioners. 2. Social norms, political agency and local responses This paper applies the conceptual framework described by Kar-

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: pplies the conceptual framework described by Kar- akislak et al. (2021). This two-dimensional framework specifies factors and variables for analysing the definitions, influencing factors, and impacts of social norms as well as perceived justice within communities. The first element of the framework proposes that three groups of in- dividuals are influential in terms of the norms that impact local

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: fluential in terms of the norms that impact local re- sponses: community spokespersons, political figures, and opposition groups. These actor groups come to power to influence public opinion in situations where there is uncertainty or conflicts, such as unequal dis- tribution of benefits. Community spokespersons have social influence and strong ties within their groups, individuals with resources

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: s within their groups, individuals with resources and networks capable of impacting others (Karakislak et al., 2021). Their opinion (e.g., support, oppose) on energy projects does not define their role, but their impact on the community and processes matters, whereas opposition groups foster a negative norm. Mayors and local council members are actors with political power, social resources, and st

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ors with political power, social resources, and strong 2 E n e r g y P o l i c y 1 76(2023)113509 ties to the community. Therefore, they have a key influence on local responses to wind energy projects (Karakislak et al., 2021). The theo- retical foundations on which the conceptual framework is based are elaborated in this section by reflecting on the interdependencies be- tween social norms, local

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: he interdependencies be- tween social norms, local political figures, and local responses. 2.1. Social norms How members of the public respond to the social and environmental changes around them is widely linked to the expected reactions of others. These conditional expectations about how people will react, or how they should react, are conditional behavioural regularities - or so- cial norms (Bic

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: behavioural regularities - or so- cial norms (Bicchieri and Mercier, 2014). Overcoming conflicts associated with energy projects requires a deeper understanding of the embedded influences and values related to the social context (Upham and Johansen, 2020). There tends to be a gap between what others typically do (descriptive norms) and what ought to be done (injunctive norms) (White et al., 2009)

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: to be done (injunctive norms) (White et al., 2009). The distinction between intention and behaviour is also relevant when estimating responses to wind energy projects (Sokoloski et al., 2018). Social influence2 within groups tends to increase the effects of normative beliefs about renewable energy technologies in general (Hübner et al., 2023; Reyes-Mercado and Rajagopal, 2017). Moreover, social no

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: -Mercado and Rajagopal, 2017). Moreover, social norms or pressure from family, friends, and neighbours, alongside political actors, have the potential to influence local responses in both directions (Huijts et al., 2012). This study addresses local responses to projects as implicit normative indicators of communities. Descriptive norms about climate change mitigation are also factors that are cons

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: e change mitigation are also factors that are considered. 2.2. The role of local political figures Local politicians are directly affected by public opinion, while in turn their engagement in wind energy projects affects local responses (Friedl and Reichl, 2016). Consequently, local politicians may have a significant impact by shaping the dynamics of community acceptance of wind en- ergy projects.

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: of community acceptance of wind en- ergy projects. Active local support for community-led projects3 strongly shapes the distribution of power and the relationships between local actors (Bell et al., 2013). As key agents of societal inclusion and local policies, the needs and views of the mayors should be better understood (Gürtler and Herberg, 2021). Young and Brans (2017) and Beermann (2009) foun

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ). Young and Brans (2017) and Beermann (2009) found from their case studies that the role of the mayor as a policy entrepreneur in implementing 100% renewable energy systems is crucial. In other cul- tural contexts, for example, a case study comparison in Japan underlined the role of shared social norms about community initiatives for renew- able energy as part of policy learning by mayoral leader

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: nergy as part of policy learning by mayoral leadership and other stakeholders (Takao, 2020). Mayors may become advocates or leaders of renewable energy projects that influence local citizens (Honva´ri and Kukorelli, 2018). Local politicians have the social and political power to influence public opinion (Busch and McCormick, 2014; Friedl and Reichl, 2016; Karakislak et al., 2021). The power of the

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: , 2016; Karakislak et al., 2021). The power of the local politicians may be applied in different ways. Partzsch (2016) explains this using three concepts in environmental politics: “power with”, which includes learning and cooperation; “power to”, which involves resistance and empowerment; and “power over”, which refers to manipulation. These understandings of power are embedded in actors, agents,

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: standings of power are embedded in actors, agents, and structures that influence decisions (Partzsch, 2016). (2018), prosumers Ford et al. (2016), community liaisons Fast (2017)). 2 I. Karakislak and N. Schneider municipality actors and the community (Schwarz, 2020; Wüste and Schmuck, 2013). Public trust in local decision-makers also impacts

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: Public trust in local decision-makers also impacts the acceptance of wind energy projects (Fast and Mabee, 2015; Titov et al., 2021). Developing trust could be understood as a chain whereby leaders first build trust in themselves, then in a process, and then an outcome (Dwyer and Bidwell, 2019). Moreover, trust between the local community and project stakeholders tends to increase when local peopl

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ct stakeholders tends to increase when local people are involved in the project development (Walker et al., 2010). The transparency and openness of local actors could also potentially influence project out- comes (Firestone et al., 2018). Christidis et al. (2017) found that the perceptions of community members and local politicians tend to differ regarding wind energy projects, which could become

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: egarding wind energy projects, which could become a barrier to their implementation. In cases when communities have a direct democratic impact on projects, such as through referendums (Bell et al., 2005), political actors have the advan- tage of being able to create open dialogue that overcomes such potential barriers. There is a potential for conflicts between the local politicians’ influence on

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: licts between the local politicians’ influence on the public and how this might affect their re-election (Friedl and Reichl, 2016). For example, Walker et al. (2018) showed that a divi- sive political context in a province could spur the rise of opposition and even create an electoral backlash. Hence, in relation to projects, mayors may avoid taking sides until they are ensured of having enough pu

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: g sides until they are ensured of having enough public support or exert power over the community. This paper explores how the positioning of mayors can impact local responses and whether having supportive local politicians is key to successful project implementation. 2.3. Local responses to wind energy projects Local responses influence the outcome of wind energy projects directly (e.g., through r

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: of wind energy projects directly (e.g., through referendums) but also indirectly through their influence on local politicians (Jolivet and Heiskanen, 2010). However, it is important to understand local responses not as an obstacle to the energy transition but rather as an aspiration to increase their under- standing (Devine-Wright, 2007). Local responses are multi-layered and dynamic and can rang

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: sponses are multi-layered and dynamic and can range from support to opposition and indifference, resistance, tolerance, or accep- tance (Batel and Devine-Wright, 2015; Walker et al., 2018). Energy-related social science research has been developing a more nuanced understanding of local responses (Walker et al., 2018) and moved away from the NIMBY (not-in-my-backyard concept), which has been critic

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: not-in-my-backyard concept), which has been criticised for labelling opposition groups as self-interested or irrational (Kempton et al., 2005; Wolsink, 2006). Discussions about individuals’ attitudes, behaviours, and responses are adapted to different concepts and theoretical frameworks (Fast and Mabee, 2015; Huijts et al., 2012). Estimating and understanding local responses also requires that loc

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: derstanding local responses also requires that local actors acknowledge and cope with the emotions associated with wind energy (Perlaviciute et al., 2018). How people feel about wind energy in general, and their environmental beliefs might not reflect how they respond to projects. Positive associations about wind power tend to be more abstract than negative ones, resulting in the opponents’ respon

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: negative ones, resulting in the opponents’ responses being more clearly elaborated (Cousse et al., 2020). Being directly affected by a project tends to be a strong driver of people’s attitudes; thus, this requires better anticipation (Russell and Firestone, 2021). Van der Horst (2007) found that only people with strong feelings against wind energy generally engage in local resistance. Warren et a

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: generally engage in local resistance. Warren et al. (2005) claim that local opposition involves a minority of people but that they receive more attention from the press. On the other hand, in locally rooted projects, some actors have the potential to foster positive emo- tions and opinions about wind energy (van der Schoor and Scholtens, 2015). In the past, most referendums about wind energy proj

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: the past, most referendums about wind energy projects resulted in project abandonment. However, more recently, decisions have tended to be pro-wind energy (Langer et al., 2016). A similar trend can be perceived in Bavaria. One explanation may be that people with personal experience with wind energy tend to be more positive about it (Langer et al., 2018). Another possible explanation is that the “

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: 2018). Another possible explanation is that the “silent 3 E n e r g y P o l i c y 1 76(2023)113509 majority” either passively supports (Schweizer-Ries, 2008) wind energy or has no strong opinions about it (Gross, 2007). 3. Research methodology The aim of this study is to investigate how local politicians and social norms influence local responses to wind energy projects. In order to do that, the

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: to wind energy projects. In order to do that, the paper analyses four case studies in Bavaria and examines the processes around these wind energy projects. In this section, we will explain our case study selection, present our methods, and conclude with the analysis. 3.1. Case studies Germany is a relevant context for examining the local dynamics of wind energy development for three reasons. First

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: f wind energy development for three reasons. First, Germany’s phase-out of nuclear and coal energy requires a substantial expansion of renewable energy. Second, Germany has great potential for wind energy expansion, and third, the deployment faces various challenges. The Bavarian state government introduced the Bavarian Energy Ac- tion Program in 2019 to spur the deployment of 300 new wind turbine

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: 019 to spur the deployment of 300 new wind turbines with a 1 GW newly installed capacity (StMWi, 2019). However, this significant goal of expanding wind energy and informational instruments has met regulatory setbacks. Since there is no national regulation about wind turbines in forest areas, each state employs restrictions on planning that steer wind energy’s expansion (Bunzel et al., 2019). Part

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ind energy’s expansion (Bunzel et al., 2019). Particularly in Bavaria, forest areas carry a large potential for wind power, but – as Ludwig and Bosch (2014) suggest – this requires alternative socio-ecological integration models. In November 2014, Bavaria intro- duced the 10H rule, which defines the minimum distance from the resi- dential areas to the closest wind turbine as ten times the turbine’

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: the closest wind turbine as ten times the turbine’s total height (Baugesetzbuch, 2014). There are recent changes to 10H that allow dropping the distance rules in priority and reserved areas for wind energy (e.g., motorways and forest areas). However, local municipalities can define exceptions to the 10H rule in their local setting through urban land use plans that allow wind energy projects within

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: d use plans that allow wind energy projects within the 10H limits. Municipal actors also have to balance the local and national interests in situations when hierarchical interventions such as the 10H rule exist (Verhoeven et al., 2022). Despite the intention to avoid conflicts, local projects still face strong opposition in Bavaria (Langer et al., 2016). In practice, the 10H rule can put additiona

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: 2016). In practice, the 10H rule can put additional pressure on local municipalities. First, the 10H rule can take power away from municipalities, and second, the exception to the rule puts pressure on them since they have to justify why a distance lower than 10H is allowed (Watson et al., 2012). This often results in considerable resistance from the local population but also from neighbouring mun

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: he local population but also from neighbouring municipalities (Langer et al., 2016). Bavaria is a particularly interesting area for wind energy due to its significant role in the German energy transition. Bavaria ranks third on the list of German federal states in terms of its ambitions and imple- mentation targets of renewable energy to create socio-technical change (AEE, 2019). Thus, it is one o

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: io-technical change (AEE, 2019). Thus, it is one of the states that prioritised the energy transition. However, the 10H rule has affected the expansion of wind energy and created further conflict. Most federal states in Germany have two types of mayors, working voluntarily or full-time, depending on the municipalities’ size. There could be multiple mayors in Bavarian municipalities with more than

The first mayor is the chairperson of the council and head of administration. The local council elects the second or third mayor from their members. The Bavarian minister from the Ministry of Economic Affairs visited the wind park with six delegates

title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects

This was the first project to be implemented with a distance to residential buildings less than specified by the 10H rule. title: The mayor said so? The impact of local political figures and social norms on local responses to wind energy projects
text: ged at the time. title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: egen, Nijmegen, Netherlands A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: Energy Community Initiatives (ECIs) present important beacons in moving to a more sustainable energy pro- Sustainable energy vision at household level. The success of ECIs, however, depends on how they are framed as socially acceptable in Community initiatives and around the locations where they are active. Using framing a

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: e locations where they are active. Using framing analysis, this paper examines the framing of three Framing urban and seven rural Dutch ECI cases in the period 1989–2017. Key questions are: how have Dutch ECIs been Netherlands framed in the regional and national press through time, concerning their (positive) contribution to the energy Urban-rural transition and their support, versus (negative) pe

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: transition and their support, versus (negative) perceptions of ineffectiveness and resistance? Second, to what extent does framing manifest spatial (urban versus rural) and scalar (regional versus national) patterns and divides? The data consists of 527 newspaper expressions focusing on six themes (economic, environmental, social, political, local-development, innovation). Results show that ECIs g

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: development, innovation). Results show that ECIs generally meet a positive framing, emphasizing economic, environmental and community benefits. However, this positivity is primarily related to the instrumental need for ECIs to prove community acceptance rather than exposing ECIs’ strategic roles in promoting the energy transition. Only in urban centres we find relatively more attention for ECIs’ t

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: tres we find relatively more attention for ECIs’ trans- formational role. We suggest that the energy transition still stands to benefit from a stronger advocacy of ECIs’ contribution notably through a better strategic and spatial framing. text: ernments and local municipal councils (Olsen and Osmundsen, 2017). As explained above, this will generally require a regional (intermunicipal) scope, in which rural space is connected to urban de- mand. Public and stakeholder support, with a regional or even national scope, should be seen as contributors to the dynamic process of social acceptance (Wüstenhagen et al., 2007). In turn, such acceptan

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: (Wüstenhagen et al., 2007). In turn, such acceptance may fuel political commitment at different levels of government (Wolsink, 2018), nurturing spatial (urban-rural) solidarities (Elliott, 2019) and planning capacities (Van Aalderen and Horlings, 2020). We will now turn to the issue of how social acceptance takes shape through framing. 3. Framing In modern, liberal societies, change depends on coo

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: n modern, liberal societies, change depends on cooperation and solidarities between manifold actors and processes (Sennett, 2012). This is based, to a considerable extent, on how actors perceive and represent each other (Goffman, 1974). For transitions, a positive framing of pio- neers and niche developments, as discussed above, is indispensable. One core process to achieve this is ‘framing’: cons

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ne core process to achieve this is ‘framing’: constituting the beliefs, values and joined stories supporting social interaction and change (Van den Brink, 2009; Van den Brink and Metze, 2006; Veenman, Sperling and Hvelplund, 2019). Frames ‘enable individuals to locate, perceive, iden- tify, and label’ (Goffman, 1974, p. 56) occurrences around them. As such, frames present sets of meanings that are

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: As such, frames present sets of meanings that are shared throughout a number of people or groups within society. According to Benford and Snow (2000, p. 614), frames are “schemata of interpretation” that “organize experiences and guide action” by bringing to presence a coherent set of interpretations, ideas and beliefs. In a transition setting, frames may help to portray actors as vital beacons o

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ames may help to portray actors as vital beacons of change warranting support and attention, or as the opposite (Van Der Schoor and Scholtens, 2015). Our study adopts two framing concepts: ‘collective action frames’ and ‘scale framing’. ‘Collective action frames’ refer to the focus, variety and coherence in the expressions addressing a specific ambition or way of action (Benford and Snow, 2000). C

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ition or way of action (Benford and Snow, 2000). Collective action frames aim to align aspirations and incentives (Eaton, Gasteyer and Busch, 2014). In our case, collective action frames primarily serve as an analytical concept, prompting us to discover what common threads and patterns media expressions on ECIs yield, along thematic and spatial lines. According to Benford and Snow, collective acti

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: es. According to Benford and Snow, collective action frames entail four dimensions (1) problem identifica- tion and direction of attribution, (2) (theme) inclusivity and flexibility, (3) interpretative scope and influence, and (4) resonance (for a detailed explanation, see Benford and Snow, 2000, pp. 618–622). For our study, we cover these dimensions as follows. Concerning identification and di- r

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ns as follows. Concerning identification and di- rection, the analysis focuses on how RE investments are thematically framed, in terms of environment, economy, society, technology etc., and how this provides meaning. Besides thematic coverage, inclusivity is limited to references about stakeholder involvement. Interpretative scope describes the basic orientation of the framing of social acceptance

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ic orientation of the framing of social acceptance. This orientation ranges from more substantive and strategic aspects covering ‘advocacy’ and ‘impact’ to more instrumental aspects covering ‘stake- holders’ and ‘activity’. Resonance, finally, is examined through the tone and ‘keying’ of 3 E n e r g y P o l i c y 159(2021)112580 expressions. Tone entails the positive versus negative stance towards

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ntails the positive versus negative stance towards the initiatives. Keying, as used here, refers to the extent expressions are more descriptive versus more activist. The descriptive variant corre- sponds to what Mooney and Hunt (2009), following Goffman (1974), characterizes as ‘flat keying’, corroborating and endorsing existing ideas, practices and aspirations. Flat keying includes the reporting

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: d aspirations. Flat keying includes the reporting of an intention, upcoming activity or impact matching institutional expecta- tions and prevailing discourses. Examples are complaints about bu- reaucracy in developing ECIs. The more activist variant corresponds to Goffman’s ‘sharp keying’, entailing explicit advocacy for more radical moves in the way we think about, invest in, and review energy pr

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ay we think about, invest in, and review energy provi- sion, for example by promoting a different lifestyle or aiming at a different future or radical independence. In the words of Dewulf et al (2004, p. 185) the significance of keying helps in analytically inter- preting and distinguishing different ways of framing ECIs in the public arena as beacons of change in the energy transition. With respe

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ons of change in the energy transition. With respect to thematic orientation, tone and keying are cross-cutting, amounting to a multidimensional characterisation of collective action frames. The second concept is scale framing, referring to the way framing engages with specific spatial domains. In the words of Mansfield and Haas (2006, p. 80): “scale frames work holistically to shape how people de

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: le frames work holistically to shape how people define the scope of problems and their solutions, which also allows them to interpret what is known and what is important”. Sites of practice absorb and employ different frames to support their (lack of) interests and actions, pitched at different spatial domains. Scale may even be decisive when it comes to identifying and defining the problem and so

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: mes to identifying and defining the problem and solutions, both “simultaneous horizontal bounding and vertical or hi- erarchical ordering” (Moore, 2008, p. 214). Referring to the former, in the words of Lieshout (2011, p. 8), “framing the issue at a particular level makes it possible to include or exclude arguments, and even other actors without literally saying so.” Here, we are particularly inte

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: terally saying so.” Here, we are particularly interested in how the significance of ECIs, both in development and generating impact, becomes framed at the national level, and how distinctions emerge across urban and rural divides. The national level may serve, in particular, to connect with prevailing policy ambitions and discourses (Kooij, Lagendijk, et al., 2018), and as an active setting for ar

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: jk, et al., 2018), and as an active setting for articulating solidarity (Devine-Wright, 2011). For these reasons, we will make an analytical distinction between frames that primarily imply the local or regional scale, and those that embrace a national to global scale. 4. Methodology and data collection 4.1. Method Our study examines framing through a media analysis. The attention for media coverag

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: a media analysis. The attention for media coverage is warranted because of its known contribution to transitions. The media is an important player in co-producing images or story-lines (Loeber, Hajer and Levidow, 2011), because it is an arena where framing and sense-making take place (Magnusson et al., 2021). Besides contributing to local and more general discourses (Holstead, Gala´n-Díaz and Sut

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives

It is important to note that, in doing so, we consider med

Not many expressions make a strategic link between impact and advocacy, which could have highlighted certain economic and local (‘livability’) benefits. Hence, on the topics of tone, scope and keying, we can conclude that both numbers and close reading signal quite a strong instrumental orientation. 5.3. Tren

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: quite a strong instrumental orientation. 5.3. Trends in the data: site and scale patterns What does a further analysis of spatial and scalar patterns tell us? This section presents five main observations: (1) a somewhat growing orientation towards stressing societal benefits (sharp-advocacy), some- times overruled by the practical need for manifesting support, (2) a spatiality of tone largely due

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: ing support, (2) a spatiality of tone largely due to technology; (3) a lacking nexus between more material and social aspects of ECI development; (4) a scalar bias, notably in political and environmental expressions, towards national stakeholders and concerns, with no attention for a regional nexus of solidarity, and (5) scale framing as a function of age. First, sharp-advocacy messages (Table 3,

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: of age. First, sharp-advocacy messages (Table 3, Annex 4) primarily stem from a selection of newer initiatives, namely Lomboxnet, DuurSaam Breda, LochemEnergie, and Grunneger Power. These ECIs manifest a sharp-advocacy share between 24% and 17%, compared with 14% for the whole corpus (Table 3). As a group, the newer initiatives manifest a significantly higher sharp-advocacy rate (16%) than the on

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives
text: antly higher sharp-advocacy rate (16%) than the one established before 2007 (11%) (Table 3). This bias indicates that sharp-advocacy is connected with the actual stage of ECI development, more than with the societal role of established projects. This tendency seems overruled, however, in cases where it is vital to report on stakeholder participation, notably Windpark Nijmegen-Betuwe (where 41 of 6

title: Noisy monsters or beacons of transition: The framing and social (un)acceptance of Dutch community renewable energy initiatives

A similar instrumental overtone can be discerned for concentrations of ‘activity’ expressions, which prevail in sites with something at stake. In Utrecht, ten cites highlight the ambition of smart grid innovation. In Reduzum, nine messages detail how expansion is blocked by provincial policy). Fi
