As a result, the study highlights that affective reactions are not related to energy sources per se, but rather to infrastructure as well as the scale of the installation, and thus provide a more nuanced picture about the previously identified “unanimously positive imagery of solar po

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: entified “unanimously positive imagery of solar power” [16, p.362]. 5.3. The important role of affect in attitude formation regarding energy infrastructure Another important element that arises from the analysis is that affect matters in developing attitudes towards an energy project. Affect was found to have a mediating role in forming attitudes towards energy in- stallations. Further, affect was

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: owards energy in- stallations. Further, affect was the most relevant factor underlying at- titudes towards a wind park, solar park, and solar rooftop installation. Specifically, the more positive the affect, the lower the level of concern about the implementation of the energy project. Also interesting was the fact that when measured at a general level, affect had a larger effect on attitudes towa

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: evel, affect had a larger effect on attitudes towards the implementation of a wind park than it did in relation to a solar park or solar rooftop instal- lation. Also, affect elicited by solar energy in general appeared to be a better predictor of the acceptance of solar rooftop installations than solar parks. The conclusion is that individuals typically thought of solar rooftop installations when

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: cally thought of solar rooftop installations when thinking about solar energy at a general level. Further, the results highlighted that the regression models that included affect elicited by the solar and wind energy installations compared to solar and wind energy in general had greater explanatory power in terms of attitudes. This suggests that the more concrete an energy project appears to an in

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: e more concrete an energy project appears to an individual, the more affect seems to matter in attitude formation. Additionally, the different sizes of the effect of affect on attitudes highlighted that the latter may matter more in the case of large-scale than small-scale installations. In the introduction, it was noted that Devine-Wright [20] highlighted that the implementation of energy technol

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: hlighted that the implementation of energy technologies may disturb emotional bonds to a place, and that we can expect that these bonds will be more strongly disrupted as the size of the energy infrastructure increases. The results seem to support this assumption. Debates concerning the implementation of energy projects may thus become increasingly emotionally loaded as the size of 8 J. Cousse pro

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: emotionally loaded as the size of 8 J. Cousse projects and the general deployment of renewable energy projects in- creases. In general, in line with earlier work [32–34], the current study highlights a negative relationship between project size and public sup- port. Future research may investigate whether many small projects rather than a few large ones may be preferred by the population. Finally

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: e ones may be preferred by the population. Finally, results show that French-speaking respondents are more concerned about the implementation of a wind park than German- speaking respondents, and the opposite holds true in the case of a solar rooftop installation. With more wind parks being planned in the French-than in the German-speaking part of Switzerland, a larger part of the French-speaking

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: Switzerland, a larger part of the French-speaking population may be in the second phase of the U- shaped curve, the phase during which the population is most critical. Interestingly, however, no differences between the two language regions in the case of attitudes towards solar parks were identified. This may be caused by the low level of familiarity or experience of the Swiss popu- lation with so

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: ty or experience of the Swiss popu- lation with solar parks. 5.4. Limitations and future research As with any research paper, there are some limitations that can the source for future research. First, the study focused on a specific geographical context. Switzerland is similar in many regards to other countries that have become engaged in the low-carbon energy transi- tion, yet it also has specifi

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: arbon energy transi- tion, yet it also has specific characteristics that may affect the research findings, such as a relatively high per-capita income, no carbon- intensive domestic electricity generation, and a low density of solar and wind parks. It would be worthwhile investigating how attitudes to solar parks compared to those of wind parks vary in countries with different characteristics, esp

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: y in countries with different characteristics, especially in countries in which citizens are more familiar with solar park installations. Second, the research measured affective reactions to solar and wind energy on an abstract level, as well as reactions to pictures of energy installations. However, debates concerning the implementation of en- ergy projects are often emotionally loaded, not only

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: y projects are often emotionally loaded, not only because of the na- ture of the energy infrastructure or its visual impact on the landscape, but also because of how project developers tackle the projects [84]. The current study investigated the acceptance of the public only and public opinion as part of the socio-political and community acceptance di- mensions of the social acceptance triangle [2

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: di- mensions of the social acceptance triangle [27]. However, the concept of social acceptance of renewables is not only made up of mutually influ- encing processes at the three levels (community, socio-political, market) but also involves different actors (project developers, policymakers, consumers) who are active at different levels [29]. Further studies could investigate how emotions are elic

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: er studies could investigate how emotions are elicited and influenced in line with the different levels of the social acceptance triangle [27]. This calls for longitudinal research that could potentially contribute to the call of Wolsink [29] to recognize social acceptance as a “bundle of dynamic processes instead of a set of actors’ positions” (p.286). Third, the aim of the study was not to predi

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: 286). Third, the aim of the study was not to predict individuals’ actual intentions to act in favour or against a specific renewable energy project, but rather to compare attitudes towards solar and energy projects in a hypothetical setting. Investigating the attitude formation process early on, however, may be very important for forecasting local acceptance or voting intentions [85]. 5.5. General

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: acceptance or voting intentions [85]. 5.5. General conclusion and policy implications Literature about the social acceptance of solar energy projects and specifically, large ones, is still scarce. Based on a representative survey in 9 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s145(2021)111107 Switzerland (n =601), the present results reveal, through a comparison of smal

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: esent results reveal, through a comparison of small- and large-size solar power installations with wind power in- stallations, that the more positive affective reactions and attitudes enjoyed by solar energy significantly decrease as the size of the two technologies is similar. While earlier studies consistently found that people prefer solar over wind power, the present study adds some nuance to

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: wind power, the present study adds some nuance to these results and hints at the fact that while large solar pro- jects may appear promising, they may face similar challenges with acceptance of the population at the local level as wind energy does. In fact, the results show that solar energy is not always preferred to wind energy, and suggests that policymakers should not only rely on public opini

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: policymakers should not only rely on public opinions polls to form the basis for policy decisions, as these may lead to distorted perceptions about the public support for renewable energy projects. They should also remain aware of how these general prefer- ences may change depending on the size of the energy projects and their visual impact on the landscape [45,87], and how these can affect peo-

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: landscape [45,87], and how these can affect peo- ple’s values and elicited emotions (positive or negative), in addition to other factors, such as distributional and procedural justice [10,19,86] and place attachment [e.g. 20,49]. Policymakers should not shy away from addressing the fact that solar energy installations may not easily be scaled up from a community acceptance perspective, as is also

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: rom a community acceptance perspective, as is also the case with other technologies deployed on a large scale. For example, poli- cymakers who hope they can avoid controversy by avoiding wind energy and prioritizing solar energy [88] may encounter similar problems with large-scale solar, or fail to meet the renewable energy targets defined in their national energy policies. Policymakers should thu

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: national energy policies. Policymakers should thus consider social acceptance issues in general and, more specifically, acceptance at the local level, and think about how to diversify the energy mix to mitigate the risk of opposition. The current results also highlight the tension involved in choosing between the deployment of decentralized versus centralized renewable energy systems, as well as

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: centralized renewable energy systems, as well as the related level of acceptance of the land use required for renewable energy systems weighed against alternative uses. Findings further show that affect is an important factor underlying attitudes towards the implementation of solar parks, wind parks, and rooftop solar installations, and that affect elicited by pictures of energy infrastructures h

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: t elicited by pictures of energy infrastructures has a larger effect on attitudes in the case of large-scale installations. Further, the study highlights that affect elicited by solar energy in general is a weaker predictor of attitudes towards solar parks than towards rooftop solar installations. This supports earlier research [16] that found that individuals imagine small solar PV installations

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: individuals imagine small solar PV installations when thinking about solar energy at a general level, perhaps explaining the gap in public attitudes between wind and solar that is found in na- tional surveys. Taken together, the findings show that as the deployment of renewables significantly increases to make Europe carbon neutral by 2050, policymakers and project developers may increasingly hav

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: makers and project developers may increasingly have to attend to the emotions elicited by energy infrastructure among the population during the different phases of the development of energy projects, and improve their communication. Attempts by policymakers or project developers to convey rational knowledge about the benefits of renewable energy projects may not lead to the intended behavioral out

title: Still in love with solar energy? Installation size, affect, and the social acceptance of renewable energy technologies
text: ojects may not lead to the intended behavioral outcomes unless emotional components are taken into account. More- over, in relation to attitudes towards renewables, policymakers should be aware that there exists the risk that any technology, if deployed on a large scale, might face resistance, even if public attitudes are initially overwhelmingly positive. 9 J. text: vecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). M. Scovell et al. Siegrist, for example, found that people tend to be more accepting of solar power than other energy technologies such as nuclear, wind and natural gas [6]. Similar results have also been found in Australia, with people tending to support the use of wind and solar over other energy technologies like hydro-power, nuclear, coal and gas [5]. Another s

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: hydro-power, nuclear, coal and gas [5]. Another study comparing levels of renewable energy acceptance across countries in Europe also found that solar power had the highest level of support of all renewable energy technologies [8]. Overall, these findings suggest that people, for the most part, like the idea of using more solar power in the future; though it is less clear on people’s perceptions

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ; though it is less clear on people’s perceptions of large scale solar farms. Furthermore, high levels of broader public acceptance does not necessarily translate into high levels of acceptance at the smaller com- munity level [9–13]. Some community members experience, or expect to experience, more of the costs (e.g., local environmental impacts) and less of the benefits (e.g., wealth creation) as

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nd less of the benefits (e.g., wealth creation) associated with an energy production technology than the broader population [12]. It is not un- common for publicly supported renewable energy projects to be met with local opposition [14]. Wind farms, for example, are strongly sup- ported at the population level but often face local opposition for a va- riety of reasons [15,16]. Few studies have, th

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: va- riety of reasons [15,16]. Few studies have, though, comprehensively investigated how communities think about, and are likely to respond to, large-scale solar farms [2,4,17], especially the role of the wider energy transition narrative on local community acceptance. 1.2. Local community acceptance of renewable energy developments Recent empirical research has investigated levels of local suppor

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: l research has investigated levels of local support for utility-scale solar energy installations and the factors that influence it. Carlisle and colleagues in a series of studies found that local support for utility-scale solar in the US is high, but varies depending on a number of factors [2,17,18]. In two studies, they found that local support for utility-scale solar in California was significan

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: r utility-scale solar in California was significantly influenced by perceived positive impacts like jobs and increased property value [2,18]. They also found that, contrary to past research [11], place-based beliefs (e.g., level of agreement that where they lived was beautiful, had a great commu- nity of people etc.) and perceived negative impacts had no significant influence on support for utilit

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: had no significant influence on support for utility-scale solar when controlling for other factors like perceived positive impacts [2,18]. In another study, using a sample from the whole US population, it was found that political ide- ology, believing that a solar farm would spoil scenery, and trust in the developers were significant predictors of acceptance of a solar farm in one’s local county [

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: acceptance of a solar farm in one’s local county [17]. Other factors like the perceived seriousness of climate change and believing that a solar development would decrease property value were not significant predictors of local acceptance [17]. These findings suggest the perceived personal and community benefits of a local solar project are particularly strong drivers of local acceptance. However,

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: larly strong drivers of local acceptance. However, the statistical models in all these studies only explained relatively small amounts of variability (between 18 and 40 % in sample) in local support, which suggests other important factors need to be included to more comprehensively explain solar farm acceptance. More recent research investigating local acceptance suggests that while communities ar

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ocal acceptance suggests that while communities are not against large-scale solar, they are more supportive of smaller scale solar applications [4,19]. Two recent studies, for instance, found that support for large ‘utility-scale’ solar was significantly lower than support for rooftop or community-scale solar [4, 19]. The findings suggest that as the size of the projects increase, the level of sup

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: he size of the projects increase, the level of support tends to decrease. Cousse also found that, contrary to past findings, people express similar levels of support for solar and wind when controlling for the size of the project suggesting that the type of technology is less important than the overall footprint [4]. One expla- nation for this finding is that larger-scale solar projects seem to be

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ing is that larger-scale solar projects seem to be viewed as an ‘industrial activity’ and thus people tend to think more about the local impacts [19]. Theories of social licence and reviews of empirical research highlight a few key factors that help to explain local acceptance of energy-related 2 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 projects [10,2

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 projects [10,20–22]. These beliefs and attitudes can be broadly cat- egorised into three main explanatory factors: (1) beliefs about a project’s consequences (2) social trust and relationships with industry operators, and (3) implicit attitudes and/or feelings about the energy technology. This section will briefly summarize these three main belief/attitude categ

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: y summarize these three main belief/attitude categories and highlight how they are usually operationalised in empirical research. One important set of beliefs that is a reliable predictor of local acceptance is how people think about the consequences (i.e., effects) of an energy project. Perceived consequences are often categorised as the perceived negative impacts (e.g., perceived risks and/or co

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: negative impacts (e.g., perceived risks and/or costs) and the perceived benefits (both local and societal). Studies that measure perceived impacts (or perceived risk/cost) find that the more people agree that a project will cause negative outcomes, the less they support it [23–28]. Conversely, believing that an energy project will bring about benefits is positively related to acceptance [29–33].

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: fits is positively related to acceptance [29–33]. There is some evi- dence to suggest that perceived individual and community effects of a solar farm (both benefits and impacts) are also important for explaining local solar farm acceptance [2,18]. However, the past research investi- gating large scale solar farms uses relatively few survey items to assess perceived impacts and benefits. As such, i

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: assess perceived impacts and benefits. As such, it is relatively unclear from previous research what types of perceived effects are important for explaining solar farm acceptance and why. Of particular importance is the extent to which people trust the company involved with an energy project or the development of a technology [20,34]. Several studies have shown that social trust is strongly assoc

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ies have shown that social trust is strongly associated with acceptance in many different contexts [26, 35–37], including the acceptance of solar farms [17]. However, the findings are mixed regarding the extent to which social trust explains acceptance in less contentious energy industries like renewable energy projects [9,23]. Associated with social trust, other psychosocial factors also seem to

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: al trust, other psychosocial factors also seem to explain local acceptance of energy projects. For example, procedural fairness, believing that the community has been fairly involved in a company’s decision making, and relationship quality, believing that there is a good community relationship with a company, have both been shown to be strongly association with trust and, in turn, local acceptance

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ociation with trust and, in turn, local acceptance [26,35]. In addition to these factors, other research finds that distributive fairness (i.e., believing that local benefits will be shared fairly) and governance (i.e., believing that governance would be effective and trustworthy) also help to explain variability of a measure of local acceptance [26]. While these factors do seem to explain local a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: 6]. While these factors do seem to explain local acceptance in contentious energy industries (e.g., mining and coal seam gas extraction) it is unclear from past research how much these addi- tional contextual factors help to explain local solar farm acceptance. Beyond more explicit beliefs about a project’s impacts and the context, affect and/or feelings tend to be another reliable predictor of en

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: elings tend to be another reliable predictor of energy technology acceptance. Affect is a broad term that can encom- pass general moods, specific emotions and feelings.1 Psychological research has consistently shown that people’s affect and/or feelings are strongly associated with explicit attitudes, preferences and behaviour [38,39]. The affect heuristic provides one explanation for this finding

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: uristic provides one explanation for this finding and posits that people tag attitude objects (people, places, technology) with either positive or negatively valanced imagery [40,41]. Put simply, people tend to form quick evaluations of things based on feelings of relative ‘goodness’ or ‘badness’ they associate with it. There is some evidence to suggest the feelings people associate with solar far

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ggest the feelings people associate with solar farms help to explain their reported level of acceptance. For example, Cousse found that one of the strongest predictors of local concern about a large-scale solar farm was the affective responses people report (i.e., ‘liking’ or ‘disliking’) when shown pictures of a solar farm [4]. The same study found that whilst people tended to think of 1 Affect i

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: d that whilst people tended to think of 1 Affect is sometimes uses synonymous with the concept of an attitude. That is, an explicit (or implicit) liking or disliking of an attitude object. The word feeling instead of affect is used in this research to differentiate it from attitude. 2 M. Scovell et al. positively valanced words when asked to think about solar panels in general, when respondents we

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: about solar panels in general, when respondents were presented with pictures of large-scale solar farms they tended to think of more negatively valanced words. The results suggest people tend to associate images of larger scale pro- jects with more negatively imbued thoughts, which in turn, lowers community acceptance [4]. These findings are supported by other research showing that people tend to

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ted by other research showing that people tend to think about solar power, and other renewables, in an abstract way, which tends to lead to more favourable evaluations [9]. However, when people are asked to think more about the potential negative impacts of a solar farm project (e.g., toxic waste generated during production), then acceptance is lower [9]. The find- ings suggests that the feelings

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: er [9]. The find- ings suggests that the feelings people associate with solar farms should help to explain acceptance. 1.3. Wider public attitudes toward the environment and energy While most studies investigating social acceptance of energy tech- nologies tend to focus on contextual factors, other research suggests that broader beliefs people hold about related topics (e.g., climate change, the e

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: about related topics (e.g., climate change, the environment, politics) help to shape how people think about energy projects [42]. Several studies have, for example, shown that pro- environmental beliefs (and/or environmental concern) and specific values (e.g., altruism) help to shape attitudes towards a range of energy technologies [16,43–46]. Moreover, these values seem to influence acceptance t

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: eover, these values seem to influence acceptance through their influence on more context specific beliefs like the perceived risk and benefit of a technology [16,29,42,47]. There is evidence to suggest that broader beliefs and values also influence local solar farm acceptance. Carlisle et al. highlights that broader beliefs about the environment and one’s political affiliation are related to local

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: d one’s political affiliation are related to local support for utility-scale solar [17]. Cultural theory posits that environmental values, beliefs, and attitudes interplay with political perspectives and about how best to address environmental issues such as climate change [48]. This interplay is reflected in broad worldviews about relationships between humans and nature, the robustness of the env

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: tween humans and nature, the robustness of the environment to change, the need for human intervention, and the role of government and technology [49]. Another important aspect that has received little consideration in past research is the extent to which people support more specific nar- ratives about the renewable energy transition [50,51]. A narrative typically addresses why a technology is need

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ative typically addresses why a technology is needed, why this technology over other options, and how this technology solution fits into a broader solution, in this case as part of a contested energy mix [50]. The narrative reflects the underlying rationale as to how effectively the technology addresses a broader problem or issue. Narratives have been identified as important in shaping public deba

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: een identified as important in shaping public debate and critical in speeding up or slowing down a transition to novel technologies [52]. The narrative around the energy transition developing in Australia has different dimensions based on the role that individual energy sources are seen to play in the economy and how they play into the trilemma of energy needs [53,54]. Australia, for example, is a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: energy needs [53,54]. Australia, for example, is a country that has created much of its wealth due to mineral and energy extraction and export. As such, energy transition narratives include implications for these industry sectors. The energy narrative also stipulates a need for energy to be affordable, sustainable and reliable for the everyday household and for the functioning of the economy. This

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ehold and for the functioning of the economy. This implies a po- tential trade-off: the willingness of households to trade-off energy affordability and reliability for a speedier energy transition. 3 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 1.4. A country experiencing an energy transition The public discussion of the energy mix of the future also need

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: scussion of the energy mix of the future also needs to be set in context of the role that energy plays in the revenue of the country and national economic benefits. Australia is an energy exporter with coal mining accounting for 1.7 % and activities from oil and gas extraction accounting for around 2 % of the gross domestic product [55] 2. Additionally, royalties contribute around $10 billion or 4

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ally, royalties contribute around $10 billion or 4.4 % of the states’ general finance statistics revenue [55].3 Almost 19 % of Aus- tralia’s product exports in 2019 were fuels [56] and the country is one of the top four global exporters of LNG and a top two coal exporter [57]. Like many countries dependent on fossil fuel related revenues, a tran- sition to the use of renewables and phasing out of

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ition to the use of renewables and phasing out of fossil fuels extraction can be perceived as a risk for this source of revenue and economic ac- tivity in the country. It is, therefore, likely that public beliefs about the broader energy system in Australia and, particularly its historical reli- ance on the fossil fuel industry for economic growth, will influence how people think about renewables

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: will influence how people think about renewables and, in turn, solar farms. Different energy resources will have different timelines for being phased in or phased out. In global demand scenarios based on stated policies and announced pledges, natural gas still appears to have a long- term role to play in the energy mix of countries and plausibly in Aus- tralia’s energy exports [58]. Perceptions a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: n Aus- tralia’s energy exports [58]. Perceptions about natural gas and its future role in Australia [53], as well as the country’s capacity to become an exporter of renewable energy should also influence views about the role for renewables and local acceptance of solar farms. Determining the extent to which such view influence local acceptance also has implica- tions for other countries outside of

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: has implica- tions for other countries outside of Australia experiencing a similar en- ergy transition who also rely on natural gas as part of their energy system. 1.5. Aims This study has two main aims. The first is to provide a descriptive representation of the social licence factors that are associated with local large-scale solar farm acceptance. Past research highlights the impor- tance of e

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ce. Past research highlights the impor- tance of explanatory factors such as perceived impacts, benefits and social trust for explaining local acceptance of energy technologies. However, the relative importance of such factors, using a comprehensive set of measures, for explaining the local acceptance of solar farms has yet to be tested empirically. The second aim of this study is to explore the e

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: . The second aim of this study is to explore the extent to which broader beliefs about the energy transition narrative may further influence local solar farm acceptance (directly and via other explanatory factors). As this study uses an exploratory data analysis approach, no specific predictions are made about the relative impor- tance of specific variables, causal mechanisms, or the validity of a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: variables, causal mechanisms, or the validity of a spe- cific theoretical model. Instead, the goal of this study is to identify robust effects in a large, representative sample to aid future research and theory development. The findings are also relevant to practitioners seeking to understand the key factors underpinning social acceptance of large-scale solar farms and how these are influenced by

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: scale solar farms and how these are influenced by energy transition narra- tives. The research is of particular relevance to those parts of the world where fossil fuel related industries still hold a key role in the economy. 2. Methodology This research used an online survey instrument to elicit a range of 2 On an industry level gross value-added basis. Figures approximate contri- butions for the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: asis. Figures approximate contri- butions for the most recent years of data available, namely FY 2016–17 and 2017–18. According to the same measure the mining sector overall accounts for around 9.5 % of the national gross domestic product. 3 Average over 2011–2020 for all royalties paid at state level. The most recent year of data is the FY 2019–20 where state level royalties reached more than $15

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: where state level royalties reached more than $15 billion and accounted for about 5.6 % of the states’ government revenue. 3 M. Scovell et al. beliefs and attitudes towards the development of a local solar farm. In survey research, the goal is to obtain a representative sample of target population to make reliable inferences about that population. This research used an undirected network modellin

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: This research used an undirected network modelling approach to determine the conditional associations between a number of theoretically relevant social licence constructs. 2.1. Participants Respondents for this study were recruited using an online research panel to obtain a representative sample of the Australian population. To recruit participants, a market research company sent information abou

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: s, a market research company sent information about the survey to Australians enrolled on their research panel. Participation in the study was voluntary for members of the research panel and par- ticiaptns were awarded points (redeemable for monetary vouchers) from the market research company for participation. In total, 3103 re- spondents completed the survey, 45.9 % male 53.8 % female and 0.2 %

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: d the survey, 45.9 % male 53.8 % female and 0.2 % who preferred not to disclose the gender or identified as non-binary. This sample size was chosen to achieve sufficient statistical power for the planned analyses, which extended beyond the analysis presented in this research. The sample was representative of the Australia population with respect to gender, age and location of residence (see Walton

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: gender, age and location of residence (see Walton et al. [59] for more information regarding the demographic characteristics of the sample). Half the sample was currently employed (50.1 %) and over a third (35.6 %) of the sample owned solar panels at home. Most of the respondents completed high school (84 %) and over a third had a uni- versity degree (i.e., a bachelors and/or postgraduate degree;

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ree (i.e., a bachelors and/or postgraduate degree; 37.6 %). 2.2. Materials and procedure Before being presented with questions assessing attitudes and beliefs regarding solar farms, all participants were presented with a hypothet- ical scenario about a planned solar farm project. Information regarding the planned solar project was presented in a mock letter from a hypo- thetical company building t

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ck letter from a hypo- thetical company building the solar farm (see Walton et al. [59] for the letters presented to the participants). In all scenarios, participants were told about the plan the build a solar farm near their town of residence, 10 kms away from the nearest residential house. Some aspects of the solar farm were experimentally manipulated (a 3 ×2 ×2 design) to test whether specific

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: lated (a 3 ×2 ×2 design) to test whether specific solar farm characteristics influenced acceptance or other psychological factors of interest. These experimental conditions had little or no effect on social acceptance or any of the other psycho- logical constructs assessed in the survey [59]. As such, the experiment conditions were not included as confounding variables in the subsequent analyses.

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: confounding variables in the subsequent analyses. Solar farm acceptance, underlying social licence factors, and other psychological factors of interest were assessed using an online survey instrument. The survey instrument was created as part of a larger research project so not all the items were used in this study (see Walton et al. [59] for a full copy of the survey instrument). Informed by the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: l copy of the survey instrument). Informed by the theory and empirical research, multiple items were created to assess a solar farm acceptance and a range of relevant social licence factors (e.g., perceived benefits, impacts and social trust). All these survey items were assessed using seven-point Likert scales, and scores on the items were summed and averaged to assess the factors of interest (se

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: and averaged to assess the factors of interest (see Table 1). Seven-point scales were chosen to increase the sensitivity of the measure (i.e., capture more nuanced responses) and to increase the variability of responses for the analysis [60]. Most of these scales used to assess social licence factors were informed by past research. Some items were reverse scored before summing so that higher score

Demographic factors were not included in the analysis as they were, for the most part, unrelated to the psycho- logical constructs assessed (see Ref. [59]). This study also assessed fac

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ssed (see Ref. [59]). This study also assessed factors that have not been included in past survey research. As discussed in the introduction, this study aimed to explore the extent to which broader beliefs about the energy transition influenced solar farm acceptance. As beliefs about the energy transition have not been comprehensively assessed in past research, 17 new items were created to assess

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ast research, 17 new items were created to assess these beliefs. To reduce the number of variables for subsequent analysis, a principal component analysis was used. The results of the principal component analysis suggested a four-factor so- lution. These factors were labelled ‘role for renewables’ in the energy transition, ‘role for gas’ in the transition, ‘role for fossil fuel exports’ during the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nsition, ‘role for fossil fuel exports’ during the transition, and ‘willingness to pay’ or trade-off for energy transition (see Ref. [59] for the item factor loadings and the phrasing of each items). All variables were treated, and therefore modelled, as continuous variables in the subsequent analysis. 2.3. Data analysis method The data were analysed using undirected network models to explore the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ed using undirected network models to explore the conditional associations between the variables of interest. While still relatively novel when applied to human data, the network approach has been used in other contexts to explain attitudes [62], psychopathology [63], personality [64] and intelligence [65]. Applied research has also used this approach to explain attitudes towards plastic [66], vac

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ach to explain attitudes towards plastic [66], vaccine decisions [67], exercise behaviour [68], environmental related beliefs [69] and responses to the COVID-19 pandemic [70]. However, to the authors’ knowledge, this is the first time that a network modelling approach has been used to explore how psychological factors influence local acceptance of an energy technology. This innovative approach can

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: an energy technology. This innovative approach can shed light on the complex factors that drive acceptance of energy technologies, which is crucial for effectively implementing sustainable energy solutions in communities around the world. Estimating undirected network models was selected as an analysis approach for two main reasons. First, network modelling is well suited for exploratory analysis.

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: modelling is well suited for exploratory analysis. Whether specific variables (i.e., nodes) will be connected and the direction of the relationships do not need to be specified a priori when estimating a network model. This is useful when a strong theory does not exist, and the goal is to, instead, identify robust phenomena (e.g., conditional associations between explanatory factors). As argued in

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: iations between explanatory factors). As argued in the introduction, there are many different factors that may influence the acceptance of large-scale solar, and the mechanism by which these factors influence acceptance are often unclear. A good example of an unclear causal mechanism is the association between trust, the perceived effects of an energy technology and acceptance. Some research argue

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: rgy technology and acceptance. Some research argue that trust causes differences in risk/ benefit perception and, in turn, causes acceptance [34]. Other work suggests that the direction runs the other way: beliefs about impacts and benefits causes changes in trust and, in turn, acceptance [26,35]. Other explanatory factors like fairness and relationship quality are also hypothesised to influence a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nship quality are also hypothesised to influence acceptance through different mechanisms depending on the theory [20,26,35]. As such, a modelling approach that relaxes the assumption of causality and specific pathways is useful in this context and can assist future theory development and hypothesis generation. The second reason for using an undirected network analysis approach is that these models

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ted network analysis approach is that these models do not assume acyclicity. That is, undi- rected network models do not assume that causality only flows in one direction (i.e., there are no feedback loops in the model). Not assuming acyclicity is useful in this context as many of the associations between psychological factors may be caused by bidirectional effects, or by un- observed (latent) var

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: rectional effects, or by un- observed (latent) variables. For example, perceived impacts may covary with perceived benefits due to independent causal effects that each 5 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 variable has on the other, or due to an unobserved latent variable (e.g., an implicit attitude towards solar technology; [40,71]). Using a mod

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: de towards solar technology; [40,71]). Using a modelling approach that does not assume acyclicity, and therefore not ruling out feedback loops and the presence of latent variables, makes it easier to identify such effects to refine existing theories and inform the design of future confirmatory research. Undirected network models also offer a unique perspective of prac- tical importance by allowing

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: perspective of prac- tical importance by allowing variables to exert simultaneous influence on multiple others. Most statistical models applied in the context of understanding social acceptance focus on a singular dependent variable and thus obscure the intricate interconnections among independent variables. This approach proves especially advantageous when exploring complex acceptance phenomena,

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: geous when exploring complex acceptance phenomena, as variables can propagate effects through a web of intricate associations that evade clear visuali- zation via conventional methods like linear regression. By modelling variable associations in this manner, it is possible to ascertain which variables have the greatest influence across the entire network. These insights carry significant practical

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: etwork. These insights carry significant practical implications for policy and decision- making, as it is possible to determine the most influential variables within a system, rather than fixating on single bivariate associations. Gaussian Graphical Models (or partial correlation networks) were estimated using the ‘bootnet’ [72] package in RStudio. A Gaussian Graphical Model is a type of undirecte

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: A Gaussian Graphical Model is a type of undirected network model that assumes a multivariate normal distribution. In its simplest form (without regular- isation or pruning of edges), a Gaussian Graphical Model represents bivariate partial correlation coefficients between all pairs of variables included in the model, conditioning on all other variables. In this study, network models were estimated

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: bles. In this study, network models were estimated using the mixed graphical model [73] function inside bootnet, which uses nodewise regressions and the lasso regularisation penalty to reduce spurious edges (i.e., weak coefficients). The nodewise estimation approach was chosen so that the explained variability in sample (i.e., R2) could be calculated for each node in the network. Graphical represe

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ed for each node in the network. Graphical representation of the results of the undirected network models were created using the qgraph package [74]. When plotted, the nodes in graphs represent each variable included in the analysis, and the links or ‘edges’ (associations been variables) can be interpreted as par- tial correlations or scaled regression coefficients (see Ref. [75] for a summary of

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: sion coefficients (see Ref. [75] for a summary of how partial correlations correspond to regression co- efficients). The thickness of the edges represents the strength of the regularised regression coefficient between two variables (nodes) after controlling for all other variables in the network. In other words, a thicker edges represent higher regression coefficients. Another way to interpret the

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ression coefficients. Another way to interpret the network graphs is to think of each node as a distinct dependent (outcome) variable in a multiple linear regression model with all other nodes being independent (predictor) variables. The spring layout was specified to create the plot, which uses the Fruchterman-reingold algorithm [76] to determine spacing between nodes in a way that reflects the r

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: spacing between nodes in a way that reflects the relative strength of associations [74]. Put simply, nodes that have stronger associations are placed closer together in the graph. The colour saturation and width of edges are scaled with reference to the strength of the coefficients. 3. Results 3.1. title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: all other beliefs in the network. 4. Discussion This research explored the socio-psychological factors that influence local solar farm acceptance using a network modelling approach. In accordance with past social acceptance research, perceived benefits, feelings and distributional fairness were all directly associated with local solar farm acceptance [20,24,26]. However, unlike similar research in

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ce [20,24,26]. However, unlike similar research in other energy domains, other social factors (e.g., trust and relationship quality) did not have direct associations with acceptance and had rela- tively weak indirect effects via typical mediating variables (e.g., perceived benefits and impacts). The findings also suggest that support for renewable energy in general (i.e. the energy transition narr

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: energy in general (i.e. the energy transition narrative) is an important factor helping to explain local solar farm acceptance. This section will expand on these main empirical findings and outline some directions for future research with reference to relevant theory. The discussion will end by highlighting some of the study’s limitations and summarising the main implications. 4.1. Broader beliefs

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: rising the main implications. 4.1. Broader beliefs about the energy transition One novel finding in this research was the relatively strong associ- ation between local solar farm acceptance and general support for renewable energy. Support for renewables had one of the strongest bivariate correlations with acceptance and was directly related to both acceptance and positive feelings when controllin

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: h acceptance and positive feelings when controlling for all other variables in the first network model. One explanation for this finding is that people tend to think about solar farms, and their benefits, in a broader, more abstract way. Put another way, it seems that local solar farm acceptance is strongly influenced by a positive attitude towards re- newables in general compared to a more consid

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: re- newables in general compared to a more considered assessment of the local impacts (both positive and negative) of a solar farm. This expla- nation is supported by past research showing that people tend to think about renewable energy technologies at an abstract level, which tends to result in a positive attitude [4,9]. As other research highlights, a positive attitude predicts higher levels o

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ghts, a positive attitude predicts higher levels of perceived benefits and lower levels of perceived risk/cost [40,41]. 8 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 network ordered from highest to lowest strength. The figure shows that believing e on other beliefs in the network. The strong link between the ‘fragile’ worldview and support for re- newabl

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: the ‘fragile’ worldview and support for re- newables also highlights the importance of a broader, and in this case more fundamental, attitude. It was found that believing that the envi- ronment is fragile, and will only be protected if there are large changes in human behaviour, was strongly associated with support for renew- ables in the network models. Although conditional associations in the ne

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: odels. Although conditional associations in the network do not infer causal effects, theory would suggest that the ‘fragile’ worldview has a causal effect on other factors as it is a more fundamental attitude. Theories that explicate the role of ‘worldviews’ and/or ‘values’ posit that they influence energy technology acceptance via their effect on more contextual beliefs the (e.g., perceived risk

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: more contextual beliefs the (e.g., perceived risk and benefits of a technology; [29,46]). The findings, overall, suggest that those who endorse this ‘fragile’ worldview tend to be more accepting of local solar farms. It is, however, yet to be seen how strong this effect remains when people have more exposure to local effects of solar farm developments. The second network model (Fig. 3) reveals how

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: nts. The second network model (Fig. 3) reveals how the ‘fragile’ world- view relates with specific beliefs about the energy transition. Fig. 3 shows that thinking the environment is fragile is most strongly associ- ated with two specific beliefs about the role of renewables. Namely, the belief that Australia should be transitioning faster and the belief that renewables will reduce greenhouse gas e

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: elief that renewables will reduce greenhouse gas emissions. As shown in Fig. 4, these two beliefs have some of the strongest effects on all other nodes in the network. The finding suggests that these two beliefs, which seem to be shaped by a specific worldview, have a strong influence on people’s overall attitude toward renewables and the energy transition more broadly. Other beliefs about the pot

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ansition more broadly. Other beliefs about the potential direct effects of using more renewables (e.g., energy reliability, energy cost and employment op- portunities) have a relatively weak influence on the network. These findings, again, support the idea that how people think about renew- ables in the abstract primarily shapes their overall attitude towards them. Although some broader beliefs ab

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ude towards them. Although some broader beliefs about the renewable energy transi- tion seem to help explain local acceptance, others seem less important. Another interesting finding was that willingness to make trade-offs was quite isolated in the network models. This is a surprising result as strong support for renewables should be reflected in an increased willingness to make personal sacrifice

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: n increased willingness to make personal sacrifices if required (i.e., pay more for electricity or experience more blackouts). This finding suggests that while people tend to have a positive attitude towards renewables, such an attitude may not necessarily translate into behaviour that will facilitate a faster renew- able energy transition (e.g., willingness to pay more for energy if 8 M. Scovell

text: illingness to pay more for energy if 8 M. Scovell et al. necessary). Future research is needed to test whether increases in price, or more unreliable energy supply, leads to lower levels of support for renewables. Such research would have significant implications for en- ergy policy. Beliefs about the role of gas and fossil fuel exports were also quite distinct in the network models. This finding

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: uite distinct in the network models. This finding suggests that beliefs about the role for renewables are uninfluenced by beliefs about other com- ponents of Australia’s current energy system. For example, believing that there is a role for gas (if it does not delay renewables) was unrelated to expressed support for renewables. This is another surprising outcome as people with pro-environmental be

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: rising outcome as people with pro-environmental beliefs tend be more supportive of renewables, but less supportive of fossil fuels [5]. This may be due to the ambivalent role of gas as both a fossil fuel and a ‘peaking’ energy source which can support a transition to renewables. The findings also suggest that if the continued use of fossil fuels does not impede the development of renewable energy

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: es not impede the development of renewable energy technologies, views about the role of fossil fuels will have a limited effect on attitudes towards renewables and, in turn, local solar farms. 4.2. Perceived impacts and benefits Further supporting the idea that abstract considerations shape atti- tudes towards solar farms was the direct link between perceived societal benefits and local acceptance

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: n perceived societal benefits and local acceptance. The results suggest that support for re- newables in general informs people’s views about the societal benefits of solar farms, which in turn influence local acceptance. However, perceived local benefits, while associated with societal benefits and distributional fairness, had no direct link with local solar farm accep- tance. It is likely that t

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: local solar farm accep- tance. It is likely that those who agree that solar farms will bring about societal benefits also tend to agree that they will bring about local benefits even though this has no direct effect on acceptance. Again, it seems that more abstract views about the wider benefits of renewables and solar farms are the primary beliefs informing people’s expressed support for a local

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: informing people’s expressed support for a local solar farm. The strong association between feelings, impacts and acceptance also warrants further investigation. While distributional fairness, role for renewables and social benefits all have direct associations with both acceptance and feelings, impacts only seems to associate with accep- tance via feelings (i.e., no direct effect). As perceived

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: a feelings (i.e., no direct effect). As perceived impacts is quite isolated from the other direct predictors of acceptance, it is possible that people’s attitude towards solar farms informed their expressed concern about the local impacts (i.e., the causality runs the other way). Put another way, if people like the idea of using renewables they will have a positive attitude towards solar farms and

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: l have a positive attitude towards solar farms and, therefore, anticipate that the impacts will be low. The causal relationship is also supported by the theory behind the affect heuristic in that the liking or disliking of an object informs one’s explicit beliefs about that object [40,41]. For example, any feelings associated from negative imagery may directly affect perceived impacts and social a

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: may directly affect perceived impacts and social acceptance of solar farms. 4.3. Other contextual factors While many other contextual factors were included in the network model, only perceived distributional fairness was directly associated with local solar farm acceptance. As distributional fairness was associated with both perceived benefit factors (local and societal) and not associ- ated with

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: rs (local and societal) and not associ- ated with perceived impacts, it may be the case that people thought more about the benefits of a solar farm in general when answering the distributional fairness questions as opposed to thinking about the local process of fairly distributing impacts and benefits. Regardless, the results suggest that believing the benefits of a solar farm will be distributed

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: the benefits of a solar farm will be distributed fairly in a region is strongly associated with local acceptance directly and indirectly via perceived local and societal benefits. It was surprising that other contextual factors like trust in the in- dustry and relationship quality were quite isolated in the network model. It is, though, difficult to claim that these contextual factors will 9 R e

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: to claim that these contextual factors will 9 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 not be important for developing local support for solar farms. Past research tends to find that the contextual factors measured in this study help to explain local acceptance of many different energy-related pro- jects [24,26,35]. However, the studies that have foun

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ts [24,26,35]. However, the studies that have found these effects tend to be conducted in regions where people have experience living near energy-related projects [26,35]. Therefore, the participants sampled in these previous studies had exposure to the social actors involved and have, therefore, had time to develop attitudes towards them. As most Australians have not experienced living near a sol

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: Australians have not experienced living near a solar farm, most would not have strong beliefs about the social actors involved or the gover- nance practices. Overall, the results suggest that people are not yet considering the social context in which solar farms will be built and operated and, instead, tend to rely more on their general attitude to- wards renewables to inform their reported level

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: - wards renewables to inform their reported level of local acceptance. The findings in this research should be contextualised in the broader setting of Australia’s energy transition progress. As there is a limited number of people currently living near solar farms in Australia, partic- ipants in this study were asked to share their views about living near a hypothetical utility-scale solar farm. T

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ng near a hypothetical utility-scale solar farm. There are known limitations to eliciting realistic responses to hypothetical scenarios [77] and not hav- ing real-world experience living near a solar farm may have made it difficult for respondents to consider the impact of contextual factors. An inability to fully understand and imagine local impacts may also explain why support for renewables see

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: ts may also explain why support for renewables seemed to have a stronger influence in the network models than the perceived local effects of solar farms. Most people, without having first-hand experience, may not know what the consequences of solar farms are and how they would affect their lives. In the future, research using hypothetical scenarios to elicit responses may consider alternative meth

title: Local acceptance of solar farms: The impact of energy narratives
text: to elicit responses may consider alternative methods for supporting the validity of the scenarios. A randomized controlled study could be used to verify changes in research outcomes. In such a study, the treatment group could receive visual cues (e.g., pictures, videos) to help them conceptualize the physical impact of large-scale solar farms or other renewable energy infrastructure. 4.4. The EU ETS and the carbon price The EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) started in 2005

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) started in 2005 is the first major carbon trading market covering around 11,000 installations (power stations and industrial plants) across EU Member states and airlines operating internally.16 The EU ETS covers around 40% of the EU’s greenhouse gas emissions, and it was the crucial legislation that contributed to the reduction of GHG emissions by 31% below 1990

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: the reduction of GHG emissions by 31% below 1990 levels in 2020, exceeding the target of 20% [58]. Further, in 2020, the target was extended to 55% by 2030, compared to 1990 levels [59]. The EU ETS adopted the cap-and-trade system, in which a cap is set on the total amount of certain greenhouse gases that can be emitted. By reducing the cap gradually, the system ensures that total emissions fall

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: lly, the system ensures that total emissions fall over time. Each allowance gives the holder the right to emit: one tonne of carbon dioxide (CO2) or the equivalent amount of other greenhouse gases, measured as tonne of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2e). Each year, an installation should surrender adequate allowances according to its emissions, and penalties are imposed on any quantity of allowance

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: penalties are imposed on any quantity of allowances missed. After paying the penalty, installations are still obliged to sur- render missed allowances in the following year. If an installation reduces its emissions, its spare allowances can be sold to another installation that needs additional allowances. The value of allowances imposes costs on GHG emissions and encourages investment in low-carbo

title: The impacts of consumer-funded renewable support schemes in the UK: From the perspective of consumers or the electricity sector?
text: G emissions and encourages investment in low-carbon and renew- able technologies. The daily price of emissions allowances is shown in Fig. 3. Phase 1 (2005–2007) was operated as an experiment, with a cap of 2096 million in 2005 [60]. text: imited literature on drivers of policy (in)coherence has tended to focus on the role of institutional factors such as interdepartmental coordination or mainstreaming and policy integration strategies [10,23]. However, a narrow institutional or technocratic focus may overlook the importance of political factors in facilitating coherence or obstructing it. Recent research has sought to gain greater

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ng it. Recent research has sought to gain greater conceptual clarity about political drivers of (in)coherence by employing a typology that distinguishes three categories of drivers: ideas, institutions and in- terests [10]. Ideas refer to “claims about descriptions of the world, causal relationships, or the normative legitimacy of certain actors” [24]. In- stitutions refer to “a cluster of rights,

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: 24]. In- stitutions refer to “a cluster of rights, rules, and decision making pro- cedures that gives rise to a social practice, assigns roles to participants in the practice, and guides interactions among occupants of these roles” [25]. Interests are understood as “material or advocacy considerations of specific sectors and actors related to pursuing their objectives” [10]. While existing literat

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ing their objectives” [10]. While existing literature has recognised the role of these categories in influencing the stringency of energy policy (see e.g. [26] on ideas and [27] on institutions and interests), less work has been done to date on how these categories may drive (in)coherence in energy policy. Strambo et al. [28], for example, contend that factors such as framing (an idea- tional fact

Renewable energy and socioeconomic inequity in Australia Australia serves as a useful case study for understanding the socio- economic dynamics of renewable energy zones as it is one of the countries, along with the United States, that has seen the greatest level of policy interest

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: at has seen the greatest level of policy interest and activity in REZ models [1].5Australia is one of the world's largest exporters of fossil fuels. Regarding the three groups outlined in section 2.1 on which our equity analysis focuses: (i) on average, people living in rural communities in Australia have lower incomes and lower levels of social support than people living in metropolitan areas [30

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: pport than people living in metropolitan areas [30,31]; (ii) Australia has a relatively low level of socio-economic inequality by world standards [32], but inequality re- mains a major issue; and (iii) First Nations people continue to experience poorer health and well-being and higher levels of energy insecurity than non-Indigenous Australians while grappling with legacies of disposses- sion and i

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e grappling with legacies of disposses- sion and intergenerational trauma [29,33,34]. In Australia, responsibility for energy and climate policy is shared primarily between state and federal governments, with local govern- ments having a subsidiary role. To accelerate progress towards its goal of 5 A key driver for the introduction of REZs in Australia was an independent review of the electricity

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: alia was an independent review of the electricity market in 2017 led by Chief Scientist Alan Finkel, which drew on the example of Competitive Renewable Energy Zones in Texas [29]. 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 achieving net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, the Federal Government aims to generate 82 % of the country's electricity from renewable sou

text: % of the country's electricity from renewable sources by 2030 [35]. The eight states and territories (including New South Wales) have an associated range of short-term climate and renewable energy targets. While electricity grids have largely developed at the state level, electricity policy is now an area of intense interaction between the Federal Government and the states. Australia's National E

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: Government and the states. Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) covers all states and territories except the Northern Territory and Western Australia. The NEM is regulated through a co- ordinated set of state and territory legislation and policy (notably the National Electricity Law and National Electricity Rules) as well as in- stitutions such as the Australian Energy Market Operator (A

text: s such as the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), which has a key role in coordinating the development of REZs across the NEM, including via its biennial Integrated System Plans [36]. All juris- dictions participating in the NEM are establishing REZs, supported by state-specific legislation and policy. Federal-state coordination on en- ergy is facilitated by intergovernmental bodies such as
