The New South Wales Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap Of Australia's eight subnational jurisdictions (states and territories), NSW has the largest population (including the largest Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander population) and the largest economy [37]. Most of the state's electricit

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: rgest economy [37]. Most of the state's electricity infrastructure – with the exception of regional distribution networks – has been partially or wholly privatised since 2010 ([38]). Despite having the smallest share of renewable energy generation after Queensland, and the largest total energy consumption in the country [39], NSW generates the largest amount of renewable energy and hosts the large

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: est amount of renewable energy and hosts the largest number of large-scale renewable energy projects [40]. In 2020 the NSW Government launched a new 20-year policy framework for renewable energy expansion (the NSW Electricity Infra- structure Roadmap; hereafter “the Roadmap”) – and associated legisla- tion (the Electricity Infrastructure Investment [EII] Act 2020). The primary purpose of the Roadm

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: [EII] Act 2020). The primary purpose of the Roadmap is to drive renewable energy expansion – particularly to avert the risk of energy shortages following recent and impending closures of ageing coal-fired power plants – and thereby accelerate progress towards the state's greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets. This is to be achieved primarily through underwriting of new large-scale solar and

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: through underwriting of new large-scale solar and wind generation, transmission and energy storage [11]. While pre-existing planning legislation and policy continue to regulate the construction of renewable energy infrastructure across the state (including outside REZ boundaries), the EII Act is a distinctive development in that it establishes a legislative basis for constructing REZs in designate

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: gislative basis for constructing REZs in designated areas of the state. Developers (or “proponents”) are incentivised to propose projects in REZs through the opportunity to tender for rights to access the grid and for Long Term Energy Service Agreements (LTESAs), which aim to provide minimum revenue streams for investors while protecting consumers from excessively high costs [41]. The EII Act's fi

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: from excessively high costs [41]. The EII Act's first objective (“to improve the affordability, reliability, security and sustainability of electricity supply”) recalls the energy tri- lemma [38], but this is only one of eleven objectives. Notably, the Act contains several objectives relating to distributive equity, including promoting local education, training, industry and jobs (section 3(1)). W

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ion, training, industry and jobs (section 3(1)). While the Act includes objectives aligned with procedural equity to “foster local community support” for investment in renewables (section 3(1)(d)) and to “promote consultation and negotiation” with Aboriginal landowners (section 3(2)(b), it does not foreground broader community engagement requirements [42]. Methods This article is based on a mixed-methods analysis comprising docu- mentary analysis, focus groups, interviews and event observation. The documentary analysis draws on clim

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: bservation. The documentary analysis draws on climate, energy and equity policies at the federal and NSW levels, as well as a range of academic literature, think tank and NGO reports, press articles and stakeholder submissions to policy processes. We also held thirty interviews and two focus groups between February 2021 and February 2023 with forty participants, principally federal, state/territor

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: participants, principally federal, state/territory and local government officials, non- governmental organisations, First Nations organisations, universities and think tanks, and renewable energy project developers. Appendix A1 provides further details about interview participants. Appendix A2 in- cludes a template interview script. The case study included a short period of fieldwork in the Mudge

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia

Policy coherence in early implementation of the New South Wales Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap This section assesses evidence of (in)coherence between energy and equity objectives in the initial phase of implementing the NSW Elec- tricity Infrastructure Roadmap. The analysis is differentiated according to the two dimensions

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: is differentiated according to the two dimensions of procedural and substantive equity set out in section 2.1 above. 4.1. Procedural equity Our analysis of procedural equity spans opportunities for authentic and inclusive public engagement at three stages of the policy process: the leadup to establishing REZs (section 4.1.1), during the early imple- mentation process (section 4.1.2) and on indivi

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e- mentation process (section 4.1.2) and on individual projects (4.1.3). We also highlight issues of coherence relating to the participation of particular social groups (4.1.4), including First Nations people (4.1.5). Initial policy implementation is coherent if efforts to expand renewable energy through the Roadmap tend to enhance procedural equity, and incoherent if those efforts either undermin

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: y, and incoherent if those efforts either undermine procedural equity or fail to address existing procedural inequities. 4.1.1. Establishment of Renewable Energy Zones Decisions about where to site REZs have important flow-on effects for the communities within them (see section 4.2 below). The EII Act requires the Minister to have considered “the views of the local com- munity” in the REZ before d

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: iews of the local com- munity” in the REZ before declaring a zone (EII Act, section 19(4)(b) (iii)). “Investor and community support” was one of five criteria used to identify candidate REZs, in addition to factors such as potential to generate renewable energy, proximity to existing and proposed trans- mission infrastructure, and implications for agricultural land use and biodiversity conservatio

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: agricultural land use and biodiversity conservation [46]. Candidate REZs were largely identified through a geospatial mapping exercise, and extent of community sup- port was primarily derived from a separate regional planning process rather than from a tailored consultation strategy. In the CWO REZ, many community members were initially unclear about why their area had been chosen as a REZ (INT12N

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ut why their area had been chosen as a REZ (INT12N, INT20B, INT24N; see also [47], p.30). One local government official commented that the council was “blind- sided” by the announcement and that the implications of the REZ were not explained in terms that would enable the council to develop an informed position on it (INT26G; see also [4]). 4.1.2. text: Community outreach and engagement across the REZ Numerous respondents stated that community outreach and aware- ness raising in the Central-West Orana REZ had been limited in the early stages of the Roadmap (roughly late 2020 through to early 2022) (INT12N, INT17N, INT26G). They pointed out that these efforts had increased over time, partly because community groups and councils had raised concerns

text: community groups and councils had raised concerns about lack of engagement but also because EnergyCo's staffing and organisational capacity had built up and COVID-19 re- strictions had eased (INT12N, INT26G). text: e onset of consultation fatigue, leading to disengagement and mistrust (INT14B, INT17N, INT24N, INT26G, [49]). text: n/co-production or deliberation (INT19G, [42]). Two notable ex- ceptions in NSW have been (i) the involvement of First Nations groups in co-designing the First Nations guidelines9; and (ii) a community-led participatory initiative in the town of Hay to identify priorities for their involvement in the South-West REZ [51]. 4.1.3. Chalaye Clean Energy Strategy, to ensure First Nations people in Australia shape and drive the clean energy transformation.” The Strategy, published in 2024, complements state-level initiatives such as the First Nations guidelines mentioned in section 4.1.5. Despite these positive de- velopments, there have been ongoing challenges in coordinating the financial contributions of the Federal Gove

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ng the financial contributions of the Federal Government and state-based schemes towards the construction of REZs, as well as varying regulato- ry requirements for community engagement across state and federal legislation [79]. 5.4. Interests 5.4.1. Energy industry interests The coherence of energy policies in Australia has long been hampered by the fossil fuel industry, which has persistently (an

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e fossil fuel industry, which has persistently (and often misleadingly) sought to portray renewable energy measures as under- mining economic growth, employment and regional prosperity [72,80]. Here we focus instead on how the interests of the renewable energy industry may influence the ability of the REZ model to incorporate eq- uity objectives. Solar and wind project developers have both commerc

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: olar and wind project developers have both commercial and regu- latory incentives to engage with communities and deliver benefits (INT12N, INT14B, INT20B, [81]), some of which are heightened by the REZ model. Demonstrating community engagement and support along with a commitment to benefit-sharing is simultaneously: a criterion for obtaining rights to operate in REZs as well as planning approval (

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: to operate in REZs as well as planning approval (INT14B); an important element in maintaining social licence (INT20B); and an opportunity for developers to stand out in a highly competitive sector (INT12N; INT20B). At the same time, interviewees pointed out that there are limits to companies' willingness to invest in community engagement and benefit-sharing, not least due to their foundational pr

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: it-sharing, not least due to their foundational profit motive as well as government guidelines which set maximum amounts for benefit-sharing [59]. Competition between developers can be a double-edged sword when it comes to benefit-sharing. A representative of a First Nations organi- sation pointed out that this competition can be leveraged to improve outcomes for communities (INT27N). However, com

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ve outcomes for communities (INT27N). However, competition can impede coordinated and higher-impact allocation of benefit-sharing resources, as some developers prefer to ensure that the benefits they provide are individually branded (INT15B). 5.4.2. Interests within and across communities The extent of alignment between communities' interests in advancing the energy transition and social equity ma

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: vancing the energy transition and social equity may significantly affect the coherence of REZ implementation. Community members have concurrent interests in securing benefits from renewable energy projects while avoiding their adverse impacts (INT12N, INT25N). One survey conducted in 2024 found that around 69 % of people living in REZs believed that “local communities stand to gain when their loca

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: t “local communities stand to gain when their local area hosts renewable energy projects if things are done right” [82], while another held in early 2025 found that people living in existing or po- tential REZs were less confident than other Australians that they will benefit from the energy transition [83]. A coherent approach is made more challenging by the fact that community members' interests

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ging by the fact that community members' interests may clash. Sometimes this may be a product of landowners competing to host a project or disagreeing with neighbours who wish to do so (INT26G, INT25N). Interests may also clash over how benefits should be distributed. The NSW Government's Benefit-Sharing Guideline identifies degree of impact and the size of affected populations as primary criteria

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: e size of affected populations as primary criteria for distributing benefits [59]. However, these two criteria may come into tension, as reflected in concerns raised by community members about larger but less affected population centres receiving substantial shares of funding. A survey of 147 respondents about the CWO REZ's Community and Employment Benefits Program found that over 83 % believed th

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: Benefits Program found that over 83 % believed that some communities should be prioritised over others in benefit-sharing, primarily for equity 8 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104415 reasons, while a majority thought that degree of impact (which pri- marily relates to smaller localities) should be the most important crite- rion [84]. However, respondents cited

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: tant crite- rion [84]. However, respondents cited a number of different principles for distribution. A diversity of views may not in itself produce inco- herence, but the procedural avenues chosen to inform decision-making (ranging from surveys of the kind used for this program to more co- designed approaches such as participatory budgeting [85]) may influ- ence public perceptions of whether the r

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ay influ- ence public perceptions of whether the resulting distribution is seen as fair. 6. Discussion and policy implications The results presented in section 4 reveal elements of coherence and incoherence for both procedural and distributive equity, as summarised in Fig. 1 below. Some aspects of (in)coherence reflect findings from previous research on individual renewable energy projects, e.g. v

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ch on individual renewable energy projects, e.g. varia- tions in the quantity and quality of consultation may influence the extent to which procedural equity is enhanced or undermined by the projects (e.g. [86,87]). The analysis also reinforces findings that procedural and distributive equity may interact to produce more or less coherent out- comes: for example, the extent of community participati

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: : for example, the extent of community participation in decisions about benefit-sharing and impact management may influence the extent to which those decisions align with public perceptions of distributive fairness [13,70,88]. Equally, the analysis also highlights the acuteness of the challenge of coherence in REZs in three respects. First, the larger number of proposed projects in a REZ increases

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ger number of proposed projects in a REZ increases both the amount of information that affected communities need to process and act upon, and the extent of institu- tional capacity needed to ensure meaningful and coordinated commu- nity engagement. Second, institutional complexity resulting from overlaying the REZ model upon existing institutions can inadvertently pose obstacles to procedural equi

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: an inadvertently pose obstacles to procedural equity by rendering the system harder to navigate. Third, the intensification of benefits and impacts in REZs opens up new opportunities but also increases risks of inequitable dis- tribution. The intensity of these challenges is underscored by the fact that while benefit-sharing mechanisms have begun to flow to commu- nities in the CWO REZ, overall pr

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: o flow to commu- nities in the CWO REZ, overall progress on the Roadmap remains behind schedule, partly due to growing community opposition [5], which may in turn have been affected by prior public engagement processes. The analysis in section 5 indicates that coherence and incoherence can be driven by a mix of ideas, institutions and interests, and that these dimensions may interact. For example,

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: d that these dimensions may interact. For example, institutional factors such as regulatory measures may change the way in which industries pursue their interests, while a mix of ideas, institutions and interests can converge to privilege large-scale, corporate-owned projects over community-owned initiatives (see also [2]). While it is difficult to draw precise conclusions about the dominance of o

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: draw precise conclusions about the dominance of one type of driver over others, Fig. 1shows that some drivers affected multiple aspects of (in)coherence, notably institutional capacity and industry interests. Some respondents argued that interests ultimately mattered more than ideas or institutions (e.g. INT14B). Even if this view is plausible (which remains a matter for debate), this does not me

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: ich remains a matter for debate), this does not mean that interests remain static or that ideas and institutions are irrelevant. While fossil fuel interests continue to obstruct a swift and equitable energy transition [89], the lower cost of renewables is shifting the configuration of industry and community interests, and our analysis reveals some potential to harness renewable energy interests no

title: Can renewable energy zones become regional equity zones? Policy coherence and social equity in the Electricity Infrastructure Roadmap in New South Wales, Australia
text: potential to harness renewable energy interests not only to accelerate the transition but also to make it more equitable (see section 5.4.1). Interviews and documentary analysis revealed a range of policy options that could enhance coherence. While space precludes a comprehensive account of these options, a selection of key recommen- dations for current and future REZs is summarised in Table 1belo

J. Lomax et al. conflict occurred or not. The study advances understanding of conflict that arises from renewable energy projects at multiple points in energy system changes.

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ects at multiple points in energy system changes. It argues that conflict mechanisms demonstrate how and when energy system changes are considered relevant to conflict actions by project developers. Consequently, conflict is not regarded merely as an outcome but as interacting with energy systems—i.e. actions around renewable energy projects can not only exacerbate but also reduce conflict. The st

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: t only exacerbate but also reduce conflict. The study aims to contribute to the theory underpinning conflict sensitive approaches and the causes of violent conflict more generally in contexts of energy development. Following a review of relevant literature, the framework of conflict sensitivity is addressed with a systems mapping approach suited for energy systems. Subsequently, a description of m

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: r energy systems. Subsequently, a description of methods and data used are detailed, along with limitations of the study. The results are set out in three sections. First, a systems map of conflict manifestations captures available evidence of violence and other conflict actions. Second, the study shows the range of conflict mechanisms reported by project de- velopers as well as in independent stu

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: project de- velopers as well as in independent studies. These conflict mechanisms are grouped into different pathways of conflict. Third, the paper ex- amines discrepancies between the project developer and analysts of independent studies in identification of conflict mechanisms. These re- sults inform a discussion of implications to conflict sensitivity, critiquing the limitations of LTWP. The fi

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: tivity, critiquing the limitations of LTWP. The final section concludes with insights on enhancements of conflict sensitivity in renewable energy projects. 2. Relationship between energy systems and conflict 2.1. Conflict escalation While existence of causal links from energy systems to violent con- flict is well established, there is less clarity on the exact nature of these causal links. There a

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: on the exact nature of these causal links. There are studies that explore these links for fossil fuels [16–18] but analysis for renewable energy is still nascent [19]. On one hand, it has been argued that energy systems can catalyse violent con- flict, notably through their role in the “resource curse” (ibid). The theory articulates how a country’s endowment in energy resources can spark violence

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: endowment in energy resources can spark violence as it facilitates the maintenance of poor governance structures [20,21]. On the other hand, while securing access to renewable energy resources may not be regarded as contentious compared to other scarce resources [22,23], it can nevertheless be a trigger of interstate violence [24,25] and throw up security concerns [26]. Furthermore, violent confl

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: security concerns [26]. Furthermore, violent conflict can be experienced when energy systems exacerbate competi- tion for land (e.g. [27]), hold potential to induce environmental degradation and affect local communities and livelihoods (e.g. [28]). In cases of wind energy, it has been argued that there can be both “man- ifested and hidden reasons for opposition” to projects, making causes of confl

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: or opposition” to projects, making causes of conflict hard to discern. Further, conflict can emerge, even after a project decision has been taken [29]. One of the reasons for this fragmented scholarship can be attributed to the vague understanding of conflict. The terms ‘violent conflict’, ‘armed conflict’, and ‘conflict’ are often used interchangeably [30]. There is no agreed-upon operational def

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ably [30]. There is no agreed-upon operational definition in research on violent conflict [31–34]. For the purpose of this paper, we draw on studies on micro-level conflict, which argue that “[i]n its most simple under- standing ‘conflict’ can be defined as a fundamental disagreement be- tween at least two actors on some issue of common concern… while one can define ‘violence’ simply as use of for

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ile one can define ‘violence’ simply as use of force.” ([35]: 14–15). Here, violence is an action, through which something is being done. In contrast, conflict is related to beliefs, perceptions and interests, and so is a state of being rather than a specific action. Thus conflict is one of several possible motivations for violence.1 Importantly, conflict may 1 In this paper, we exclude individual

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: onflict may 1 In this paper, we exclude individualised criminal violence, petty theft and so on and consider that conflict may exist independently of such violence. 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 100(2023)103089 also be manifest in actions other than violence. There are diverse ways in which conflict can manifest, identified in studies on models of conflict escalation [3

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ied in studies on models of conflict escalation [36–38]. Here, we use a condensed and adapted form of the conflict escalation model by Yasmi et al. [38] to frame analysis of conflict manifestations: i) Conflict communication incorporates all of Yasmi et al.’s [38] actions involving exchange of information: ‘debate and critique’, ‘lobby and persuasion’, and ‘protest and campaigning’; ii) Use of jus

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: on’, and ‘protest and campaigning’; ii) Use of justice services incorporates court action but also other third party involvement in justice and restitution, such as in traditional conflict resolution mechanisms [39]; iii) Restriction of access includes actions that impose costs on others but fall short of causing direct physical harm. This includes roadblocks or other such restrictions of freedom

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: roadblocks or other such restrictions of freedom of movement; iv) Violent actions include actions taken that either cause physical damage to, or else appropriate, people or property. This includes killing or injuring, sexual violence, arson, theft of property, and so on. The violent actions of in- terest are those that are group-based and motivated by conflict, rather than criminal violence. 2.2.

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: d by conflict, rather than criminal violence. 2.2. Conflict sensitivity Being attentive to such conflict and its escalation is pertinent to renewable energy projects because they highlight the implications of foreign direct investment and the role of the private sector [6,7], amidst efforts towards addressing climate change. It is important to private sector investors because of both reputational

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ate sector investors because of both reputational and operational risks [40]. Where energy projects are donor-funded to support development, con- flict sensitivity is additionally important. Scholarship on conflict sensi- tivity regards conflict as “the result of a disagreement between actors on the basis of perceived incompatible goals” [41], adapting [42]. Where the goal is development, impacts

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ting [42]. Where the goal is development, impacts on existing conflicts as well as creation of new conflicts are serious mitigating factors. For accountability as well as for improving practice, it is important for donors and implementers using those donor funds to transparently analyse and communicate their impact on conflict. Contrary to current scholarship that focuses on existing conflict, by

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: scholarship that focuses on existing conflict, by employing a conflict sensitivity lens, it is possible to understand the potential of projects to generate new conflict as well as to interact with existing conflict. This approach advances existing studies that analyse how energy projects result in conflictive outcomes [43,44] and further makes it possible to consider how energy projects may be the

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ossible to consider how energy projects may be the cause of reduction in conflict. Conflict sensitivity encompasses a range of ap- proaches to reduce manifestations of conflict. Seminal work on conflict sensitivity focuses on the principle of ‘do no harm’ and emphasises the importance of enhancing local capacity to deal with conflict [45]. This requires a deep understanding of the local context, a

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: uires a deep understanding of the local context, across a range of aspects including systems and institutions, shared values, attitudes, ex- periences and culturally understood symbols relating to peace and conflict. The study on conflict sensitivity also suggests that there are patterns of aid and development initiatives interacting with conflict which need to be understood and anticipated. Analy

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: which need to be understood and anticipated. Analysis of the inter- vention itself provides clarity on ways to reduce negative impacts to conflict whilst enhancing impacts that go towards abating conflict. Studies also indicate that hypotheses of causal links between develop- ment intervention and conflict (and reduction of conflict) need to be examined. In other words, considering “possible negat

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: mined. In other words, considering “possible negative and positive effects” in a participatory manner with the community in which the development intervention is to occur is valuable to provide joint deci- sion and ownership of actions ([46]: 76). Studies by donors and devel- opment agencies in which conflict sensitivity frameworks have been applied to the Turkana region examine the multiple ways

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: d to the Turkana region examine the multiple ways aid may impact conflict by utilising baseline survey, stakeholder mapping, resource-use mapping, conflict mapping and heavy use of participatory approaches [47]. 2 J. Lomax et al. 2.3. System mapping and conflict Applying the conflict sensitivity framework to renewable energy contexts requires an analysis of how implementers identify conflict; asse

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: alysis of how implementers identify conflict; assess their business operations interacting with conflict; and consider avoiding negative and maximising positive impacts on conflict, including avoiding generating new conflicts [6]. To better operation- alise this framework for energy systems, this paper follows the ‘Actions & Actors’ approach to system mapping [44,48], which focuses on the structur

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: tem mapping [44,48], which focuses on the structural interconnections within an energy system that demonstrates a range of causal pathways through which change happens. The system is mapped around one or more ‘core’ actions and associated resources.2 In mapping the systems associated with energy projects, these core actions could include the production, distribution, sale, and use of electricity.

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: tion, distribution, sale, and use of electricity. Prior and subsequent to these actions, there are land acquisition, con- struction of electricity generation facilities, use of electricity, and consequential environmental damage from using electricity etc. These are added to the system as additional actions in the stylised sequence. Note that this system mapping comprises a stylised sequence of ac

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: system mapping comprises a stylised sequence of actions of a system, and the sets of actors that perform these actions. This approach is rooted in the practitioner-oriented Mechanisms of Social Change language for analysing systems, which comprises a novel ty- pology of action that permits consistent analysis of actions with both positive and negative outcomes. This is particularly appropriate for

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ive outcomes. This is particularly appropriate for application to contexts of conflict, both in increasing or reducing in- tensities of conflict [49]. This system mapping has been previously applied to a case study of mini-grids in Kenya, which examined conflict by analysing the causal relationships between various impacts of mini-grids on local commu- nities in Turkana County [44]. The analysis o

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: mmu- nities in Turkana County [44]. The analysis of conflict impact used Sovacool et al.’s [43] concept of entrenchment, which focuses on inequality between groups. In this paper, we apply the system mapping to large-scale projects and examine how reported conflict by project implementers relate to energy system changes. The mapping enables systematic categorisation of positive and negative impact

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: tic categorisation of positive and negative impacts on conflict, as reported by the scholarship on the LTWF. The purpose of the mapping is to better show the project dimensions which implementers consider as interacting with conflict and thus clarify their understanding of conflict mechanisms. Consequently, the system mapping is not a diagnosis of conflict trends or grounded empirical analysis of

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: conflict trends or grounded empirical analysis of conflict causes, which would have required extensive field data collection beyond the sec- ondary literature used in our study, as exemplified further below. The benefit of the system mapping is to highlight potential blind spots in conflict sensitivity by implementers. 3. Research design In analysing causal connections from energy projects to viol

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ng causal connections from energy projects to violent conflict this paper follows a case study approach, which is suited to analysing complex causal links or pathways [50,51]. Within case study research, system mapping is one of a range of related methods for analysing causal relationships within complex systems, including energy systems [52]. 3.1. Case of Lake Turkana Wind Farm LTWF is a 310 MW w

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: Case of Lake Turkana Wind Farm LTWF is a 310 MW wind energy project of 365 turbines in Marsabit County, close to the south-eastern shore of Lake Turkana built between 2014 and 2016. It was financed through debt, equity, and preference shares with investors and involved a number of financiers including the European Investment Bank, the African Development Bank, the Netherlands Development Finance

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: lopment Bank, the Netherlands Development Finance Company and others [15]. Its 2 This is in line with the ‘market systems’ approach theorised by Springfield Centre [78] 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 100(2023)103089 blended finance model is representative of official development assis- tance in renewable energy [53]. Nevertheless, the project is considered as an example

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: rtheless, the project is considered as an example that demonstrates high planning risk to electricity financing in places like eastern Africa, with significant delays in achieving financial closure [54]. It is operated by LTWP, which repre- sents a consortium of operators including Vestas, Siemens, SECO and Civicon. There are four main project components, namely the wind farm itself, the access ro

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: onents, namely the wind farm itself, the access road from Laisamis, the power line, and the ‘Winds of Change’ corporate social responsibility foundation. We focus on three of these, excluding the power line which is owned and operated by Ketraco rather than LTWP. Existing scholarship has looked to this case as an example of renewable electrification through the interaction of local capacity and fo

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: n through the interaction of local capacity and foreign investors and companies [55]. The project also exemplifies the role of international IPPs who actively play a part in energy production in Kenya—and more widely in the African continent [13]. Yet the project is located in a region that has experienced violent conflict and insecurity [56]. Livelihoods in the region have involved pastoralism an

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: lihoods in the region have involved pastoralism and the area around the wind farm has a long history of violence, especially based on cattle rustling and associated killing and injury, as well as violence used to exert control over water and other scarce resources [57]. While there are many ethnic groups, these instances are exem- plified in the prominent violent conflict particularly between the

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ominent violent conflict particularly between the Tur- kana and the Samburu, relying on pastoralism. The fraught social fabric of the communities is against a backdrop of marginalisation and under- development in the region, which makes opportunities for employment and poverty-alleviation significant. As such any impacts of the project on violent conflict are important to understand and have been

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: onflict are important to understand and have been the subject of numerous research efforts, as well as critique by international civil so- ciety groups, such as Danwatch on business ethics [58]; and by Friends of Lake Turkana, a community association, on social and environmental injustices particularly to indigenous communities [59]. Some studies indicate significant reductions in perceived levels

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: ndicate significant reductions in perceived levels of violence since the project [57]; others analyse new instances of inter-ethnic group violence and thus increase of violence [60]. Clearly, various aspects of the project have faced social opposition and are the source of contention and claims of injustice. This case provides a substantial amount of secondary data, collected over a number of year

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: of secondary data, collected over a number of years using various methods. Data collection and conflict analysis were undertaken by LTWP and related actors, as well as by independent researchers. Research on conflict and in areas affected by conflict is widely recognised as challenging [61,62], and so the availability of existing data is valuable. For the approach used here, such data makes LTWP a

title: Does renewable energy affect violent conflict? Exploring social opposition and injustice in the struggle over the Lake Turkana Wind Farm, Kenya
text: For the approach used here, such data makes LTWP a useful case for analysis. The data collection efforts allows us to examine these studies’ approaches to understanding conflict impacts. 3.2. Introduction Over the past decades, renewable energy controversies have received attention from scholars from multiple academic fields within social science [1], such as environmental psychology [2–4], sociology [5–8], ethics [9–12] and policy and planning [13–18]. In studying such con- troversies, researchers have a tendency to focus on opponents, the public and

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.

This tendency borders “obsession” [28] and has resulted in the neglect of other key actors in the energy domain relevant for understanding con- troversies [3,19]. This predominant focus on specific actors and neg

In this type of conflict, a multitude of different public and private actors with various roles, representing different, potentially conflicting interests interact [17,18,29–31]. Walker et al. describe RET actors as “a broad category of people in organizations with roles in supporting or implementing [renewable energy technology] developments – including developers, consultants, PR and marketing companies, trade associations, financiers and technology manufacturers” [[17], pp. 4–5]

Interactions between RET actors and opponents are “potentially significant in shaping how the dynamics of local responses evolve and how different actors learn, react and strategically behave in relation to each other” [[17], p. 5]. In these interactions, it is not just the choice of a particular renewable energy technology that can trigger or in

text: renewable energy technology that can trigger or influence a controversy: processes of public participation and formal decision- making on renewable energy can also incite public opposition [17,32,33]. For example, processes based on Not In My BackYard (NIMBY) assumptions often leave local stakeholders feeling powerless and disillusioned by formal processes of engagement [34]. Cuppen example is the “decide-announce-defend” (DAD) model: “when results developed by ‘educated experts’, project developers or government are simply communicated to the public” [[35], p. 252]. Both NIM

text: mmunicated to the public” [[35], p. 252]. Both NIMBY as- sumptions and DAD approaches can result in (increased) public oppo- sition [33,36,37]. This means that decisions and choices, and underlying assumptions, made by RET actors on how to engage with public oppo- sition can teach us valuable insights about (the development of) con- troversies and related dynamics of interaction [17]. text: related dynamics of interaction [17]. Better understanding of controversies is crucial as many have led to delays or cancellations of renewable energy projects [33,38–40]. How- ever, such an understanding cannot be based on research into only the public, local residents or those opposing renewable energy projects. The complex nature of controversies also requires in-depth investigation into and un

title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: s also requires in-depth investigation into and understanding of RET actors, of the roles they play and their as- sumptions and expectations underlying decisions and actions [17,41–43]. Such insights create opportunities for researchers to pro- vide diverse and concrete recommendations for inclusive decision- making in energy controversies, such as recommendations that are more tailored to the cha

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: recommendations that are more tailored to the challenges RET actors face in this area. There have been calls for some years now to broaden the scope of social science research to include RET actors [19,42,44,45]. Neverthe- less, attention for RET actors still seems to be limited and it is unclear where this area of research currently stands. Such insight is necessary for developing a substantiate

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: insight is necessary for developing a substantiated research agenda. Therefore, we present a systematic literature review and ask the question: What do we know of RET actors when it comes to controversies surrounding renewable en- ergy technologies and infrastructure? Our review is restricted to studies that cover energy technologies for production and transport of renew- able energy. This include

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: and transport of renew- able energy. This includes technologies like wind, solar and hydro- power, as well as the (re)development of infrastructure, such as transmission lines that are necessary for energy system transformation. Below, we will first discuss the method we used for this literature review. Then, in Section 3, we present our findings. We identified two categories in existing social s

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: We identified two categories in existing social science research: (1) RET actors' perceptions of public opposition and (2) RET actors' responses to public opposition. Next, in Section 4, we discuss three observations related to the nature of the generated insights and characteristics of research approaches of the reviewed publications. In our conclusion (Section 5), we make a case for future soci

Results that did not address all four building blocks were excluded from the review, such as those publications that: - Do not concern public opposition, but address conflict between governmental actors or between private actors; - Strictly focus on other perspectives than those of RET actors (com- munities, opponents, etc.) related to the planning and devel

For several articles, the abstracts met all the requirements, but screening of the full text showed otherwise. This was often the case for results focusing

These results were only included if the processes did take place in the context of public opposition and included for example perspectives, rationales or discourses of RET actors. Analysis of publications included in review Next, the full texts of all publications included in the review were coded using ATLAS.ti (version 9.1.4.0). We used an inductive coding approach to allow bottom-up generation of codes [54], as the goal

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: w bottom-up generation of codes [54], as the goal was to create an overview of social science literature on RET actors, without limitations from theoretical frameworks. Although our coding approach was inductive, it was sensitized by our main research question. As such, our coding was guided by the following questions: how do RET actors think about public opposition? How do they engage with or res

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: public opposition? How do they engage with or respond to public opposition? Why do they engage with public opposition in a particular way? In addition, we allowed other topics to emerge as part of the bottom-up approach and assigned codes concerning investigated actors, renewable energy technology or infrastructure and geography. The impact of public opposition according to RET actors The literature reports on different assumptions and discourses of RET actors with regards to public opposition. Most RET actors have negative assumptions: public opposition is perceived as a barrier to the devel- opment of renewable energy projects [43,48,57,58]. A small number of publications report on RET actors

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: small number of publications report on RET actors who perceive opposition as neutral or positive. For instance, some developers find wind energy resistance movements to have a positive impact on a development, as they increase developers' accountability and “in some cases [secure] other benefits for the community that would not have been there otherwise” [[43], p. 7]. Walker et al. [58] show how i

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: rwise” [[43], p. 7]. Walker et al. [58] show how industry and policy actors construct imaginaries of the public from first-hand and mediated experience and knowledge; they describe the impact of these imaginaries as a “shared expectation of an ever present latent but conditional public hostility to renewable energy project development [that] is seen as shaping the material forms of the technologie

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: n as shaping the material forms of the technologies, their evolving spatiality, and prac- tices of public engagement involved in obtaining project consent” [p. 931]. Cass and Walker [59] find both neutral and negative perspectives on opponents of renewable energy developments among developers and other industry actors: the negative perspective views opponents as highly emotional, unreasonable and

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: s opponents as highly emotional, unreasonable and illegitimate, while the neutral perspective views such responses “as understandable and an inherent part of the politics around proposals for developments such as wind farms” [p. 68]. The authors find that, despite the different perspectives, developers perceive emotions of opponents as needing to be managed to keep the decision-making process “une

text: ject, governmental authority in decision-making and type of technology [36,59–65]. RET actors feel that causes related to commu- nity engagement and public participation play a particularly important part in (triggering) public opposition. According to a survey among representatives of national wind energy associations and experts in Europe, inadequate public participation is a reason for oppositi

text: uate public participation is a reason for opposition to wind energy projects. At the local level, for example, “the less influence actors have the more likely it is that problems with social justice, participation and conflictive situations can arise” [[66], p. 321]. Causes of public opposition according to RET actors: specific groups of people RET actors, specifically wind energy developers, industry represen- tatives (including a cooperative) and local government off

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: (including a cooperative) and local government officials, think in many cases that public opposition originates only from a small group of people [19,43,48,58,69–74]. Both local governments and developers see small groups of opponents as a particular challenge when balancing different local interests [72]. These small groups of vocal opponents are often perceived as coming from outside of host com

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: often perceived as coming from outside of host communities and stirring up local opposition [43,67]. Multiple articles elaborate on this [19,43,58,69,70,72] and show that RET actors refer to the majority of residents as supportive, to explicitly contrast that to the image of small vocal groups of opponents. RET actors perceive this majority as silent or apathetic, not actively expressing any type

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: nt or apathetic, not actively expressing any type of opinion: “Passive local people are often described by authorities and developers as lacking in- terest in the process, or as a majority quietly in support of the project - acceptance is often measured in terms of activity” [[70], p. 700]. Thus, public silence is seen as equal to acceptance [70]. NIMBYism and knowledge deficits are also common ca

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: NIMBYism and knowledge deficits are also common causes of public opposition according to RET actors [75]. Although few UK developers explicitly label opponents as NIMBYs, “self-interest was often considered to be at the root of opposition” [[19], p. 250]. Scherhaufer et al. [76] also report on public opposition explained as driven by self-interest, specifically in the case of environmental impact

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: specifically in the case of environmental impact assessment (EIA) pro- cedures. In addition to the NIMBY label UK developers also ascribed knowledge deficits to opponents: “[they] were not so much character- ized as having insufficient knowledge as having incorrect knowledge. Developers tended to characterize opponents as having faulty knowl- edge, rather than simply lacking information” [[19], p

title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: , rather than simply lacking information” [[19], p. 251]. Though there is antipathy toward the use of the NIMBY model among National Grid representatives in the U.K. [65], most publications concern exam- ples of discourses revolving around the attribution of the NIMBY label and knowledge deficits to those opposing developments.
