And in some cases, RET actors postpone or cancel pro- jects in the face of, or in response to, opposition [58,127–130], or withdraw financial support from controversial projects [131]. 4. Discussion Section 3 provides an overview of existing empirical social science research on RET actors in controversies surrounding renewable energy technolo

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ontroversies surrounding renewable energy technologies and infrastructure. To answer our research question, we now know that this body of literature mostly focusses on two main categories. The first category (Section 3.1) concerns research on RET actors' perceptions of public opposition. These perceptions relate to the nature, impact and causes of public opposition, including specific groups of pe

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: public opposition, including specific groups of people and processes of engagement and decision-making. The second category (Section 3.2) describes research on different responses and strategies of RET actors to public opposition, which serve different purposes, including preventing, reducing and delegitimizing public op- position. On their own, these categories are helpful as they provide an over

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ese categories are helpful as they provide an overview of the type of research that has been conducted. Reviewed together, there are several observations to be made about this body of literature. Below, we discuss three characteristics regarding the di- versity of the investigated actors, technologies and geography, the general descriptive nature of RET actor research and the type of prac- tices t

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: f RET actor research and the type of prac- tices that is mostly reported on. We end this section by discussing the implications of the reviewed literature for the broader context of research on energy controversies. 4.1. Little diversity in social science research on RET actors Our methodological choice to only include English publications can be of influence on the diversity of the discussed publ

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: f influence on the diversity of the discussed publications: for example, had we included publications in other languages, we might have seen differences in the investigated countries, technologies, RET actors and journals. That being said, the included English publications do not reflect the diverse character of the renewable energy domain in terms of investigated actors, renewable energy technolo

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: of investigated actors, renewable energy technologies and infra- structure, and geography. The definition of RET actors that is used in this article describes a diverse group of actors in diverse organizations working on the implementation of renewable energy. However, in the discussed publications RET actors are often presented as a homogeneous and uniform group with similar interests, goals and

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: nd uniform group with similar interests, goals and discourses. Aside from the terms “developer” or “government”, relatively little distinction is made within the RET actor category, the roles these actors take on or how they act in the context of public opposition (see also Fig. 1). There is little distinction between actors representing different departments (technical, legal, stakeholder engagem

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: departments (technical, legal, stakeholder engagement) of organizations developing projects, different types of organizations (public, private, etc.) or con- sortia consisting of different organizations. The discussed publications also show little diversity in terms of the investigated renewable energy technologies and infrastructure: up till now the dominant focus has been wind energy, with littl

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: he dominant focus has been wind energy, with little attention for other renewable energy technologies or infrastructure (Fig. 2). The striking difference between the number of publications on wind and other technologies, suggests that wind is more contentious. However, recent years have seen growing local opposition toward (plans for) solar 8 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 91(2022)102749 projects. As such, this difference could simply be explained by the sheer amount of global installed wind capacity (564GW in 2018 [132]), as well as the relatively large number of (controversial) wind farms available for investigation. The geographical focus of the publications is also characterized by little diversity: five individ

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: so characterized by little diversity: five individual countries are investigated more often than five times (Fig. 3). Well-established research groups either located in or with a geographical preference for these countries could explain these higher numbers. Or perhaps it is related to what Dai [93] points out: “issues around public participation in energy transitions in non-Western democratic con

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: n energy transitions in non-Western democratic contexts have been an under-researched area” [p. 53]. Most articles are found in journals related to policy and planning (Fig. 4), which in itself is not surprising. What is notable is the limited presence of business, development or management-oriented journals. For example, we expected to find more research revolving around con- cepts such as social

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: esearch revolving around con- cepts such as social licence to operate (SLO) and corporate social re- sponsibility (CSR). These concepts are widely researched in the non- renewable energy industry and the extractive industry (for example [133–135]). SLO and CSR practices in the renewable energy industry are investigated to a much lesser degree [135]. Future research needs to diversify in terms of r

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: . Future research needs to diversify in terms of renewable energy technologies and geographical contexts. This also opens up opportu- nities for comparative research: the publications included in this review offered a sample that was too small to make a meaningful comparison for example of the differences in technologies in relation to public op- position and the responses of RET actors. Future re

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: osition and the responses of RET actors. Future research could inves- tigate questions such as: do RET actors' practices differ between different types of organizations (multinationals with no local connections, local energy cooperatives, etc.), energy technologies or geographical con- texts? Comparison of RET actors' practices in projects with and without public opposition as well as comparison o

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: without public opposition as well as comparison of RET actors with non- renewable energy actors are also interesting research avenues. Little is also known about the development of practices and assumptions over time: to what extent do RET actors (try to) learn from public opposition? Are there changes in their practices regarding (the design of) public participation or perspectives on the public

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: public participation or perspectives on the public and public opposition? Such research requires methods for comparative and longitudinal case studies. In order to unpack the diversity of RET actors and their practices in the context of controversies, we need to gain a better understanding of who these RET actors and the organizations they represent actually are. Projects are often developed by di

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: t actually are. Projects are often developed by different types of consortia (for example, public-private) where different interests and values meet. Which de- partments are involved and what kind of decision-making power do they have regarding (interaction with) public opposition? What are RET ac- tors' discourses, assumptions, expectations and practices in the context of energy controversies and

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: actices in the context of energy controversies and public opposition? What are their ap- proaches to the planning and development of renewable energy? Such questions help build a more fine-grained and rich understanding of RET actors that goes beyond current generic descriptions and classifications such as “project developer” or “government”. 4.2. Descriptive nature of RET actor literature What is

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: Descriptive nature of RET actor literature What is also notable is that the majority of publications has a descriptive nature: actions, practices or discourses of RET actors in response to, or in anticipation of, public opposition are described, but the majority of results do not provide an explanation. For example, Landeta-Manzano et al. [83] describe industry practices that are designed to achie

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ribe industry practices that are designed to achieve community acceptance of wind farms and simul- taneously avoid conflict. However, in this publication, motivations or rationales underlying these practices, or explanations of why these practices are successful (or not), are not investigated. A reason for publications' descriptive nature might be that the majority of them concern case studies in

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: that the majority of them concern case studies in which controversies are investigated from the perspective of multiple actors involved, including RET actors (see 8 E. van de Grift and E. Cuppen Fig. 1). These multi-actor analyses often also have a strong focus on local communities or opponents and their experience of the controversy, while briefly reporting on RET actors. This means there is litt

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: reporting on RET actors. This means there is little room for in-depth investigation of RET actors and rationales underlying their responses to public opposition. As such, there is a need for more explanatory research on RET actors and their practices. RET actors make assumptions and have expectations about the public, opponents, and their responses to renewable energy technologies and the plannin

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: s to renewable energy technologies and the planning process, which also shape RET actors' practices in terms of: “how they ought to operate, how they do operate, and how they can and should engage with the public” [[17], p.7]. Explanatory research can provide insights into how these actions and interactions of RET actors in energy controversies come about. What rationales do different types of org

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: e about. What rationales do different types of organizations (public, private) have for their approach to public opposition, and how do these shape public engagement practices [17,136,137]? Research that explores relation- ships between different categories of RET actors and develops an un- derstanding of networks of RET actors and interactions between them is also lacking. Disciplines such as org

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ween them is also lacking. Disciplines such as organizational studies, organizational anthro- pology and business administration and methods such as ethnography [138–140] can facilitate an explanatory understanding at inter- and intra-organizational levels. Questions related to organizational culture, identity and power dynamics [141,142] can help gain insights into intra- organizational dynamics

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: gain insights into intra- organizational dynamics and subsequently into explanations of (engagement) practices and interactions in the context of public oppo- sition. For example, what kind of intra-organizational constraints and enablers do they experience when public opposition is anticipated or encountered [44]? How do organizational disagreements about (the level of) accommodation of public in

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ts about (the level of) accommodation of public interests and concerns affect de- cisions on engagement with opposition? 4.3. Little research on RET actors accommodating public concerns Most of the identified responses show RET actors trying to avoid or reduce public opposition. Or in a more direct way, they attempt to oppress or restrict it. Section 3.2.8 provides some examples of RET ac- tors ad

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: on 3.2.8 provides some examples of RET ac- tors adapting projects or processes to accommodate public concerns. Nevertheless, this review has unearthed little research that investigates this type of responsiveness of RET actors toward public opposition in controversies. This is interesting, as there are more and more pleas for learning from public opposition and approaching it as a source of in- fo

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: pposition and approaching it as a source of in- formation about the diversity of normative appraisals in renewable en- ergy planning [18,30,143]. These pleas are coming from academia, as well as practitioners. One example of RET actors attempting to engage with public opposition in a responsive way, that we know of from the Dutch context, is the Energy and Environment Learning Platform.6 This comm

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ergy and Environment Learning Platform.6 This community of practice, initiated by the energy industry and the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Climate, is geared toward knowledge exchange on community engagement in the energy transition. Other examples are voluntary codes of conduct on stakeholder engagement in renewable energy development, like the one created by the Dutch Association of Geotherm

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: e one created by the Dutch Association of Geothermal Operators [144]. However, such initiatives and practices of RET actors are not reported in this literature review. Perhaps the rela- tively slow speed of scientific publications is still catching up with such evolving practices in the world of energy transitions. 4.4. Implications for the broader context of research on energy controversies Follo

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: context of research on energy controversies Following the results discussed above, we share two observations that shed more light on the investigation of RET actors in the broader field of renewable energy controversies and its conflict dynamics. 6 LEO, in Dutch Lerend Platform Energie en Omgeving, www.platformleo.nl. 9 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 91(2022)102749 The fi

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 91(2022)102749 The first observation we made while conducting this literature re- view is that some researchers explicitly highlight a bias in their research on RET actors [43,80]. For example, Hall et al. [80] use documents from wind energy developers to: understand the wind industry's current thinking, practices and trends in community engagement and benefit sharing.

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: rends in community engagement and benefit sharing. While we recognise a bias in this perspective, and note that research has recommended the benefits of holistic insights from multiple stakeholder perspectives and experiences [43], these strategic documents are rarely shared externally and provide a unique developer-centric perspective that is rarely accessible [p. 125]. Although Hall et al. do ex

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ly accessible [p. 125]. Although Hall et al. do explicitly state the value of this developer- centric approach, it is preceded by a disclaimer. One could ask why developer-centric research is explicitly labeled as biased, while most research on opponents, communities and residents engaged in energy controversies does not explicitly receive such a label. This creates the impression that investigati

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: abel. This creates the impression that investigation of certain actors is somehow equal to being their ally or championing their interests. We should follow the lead of Burningham et al. [19], Cass and Walker [59], and Hall [74], and be cautious “not to vilify developers or to romanticize project opponents (Lake 1993, Wexler 1996)” [[19], p. 258]. The second observation was made while we determine

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: The second observation was made while we determined the eligi- bility of publications for this literature review. During this process, we noticed quite a number of articles and chapters that investigated prac- tices of RET actors through the eyes of other stakeholders. Often these publications concerned stakeholders who were affected by RET actors' practices, such as residents and opponents [48,55

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: practices, such as residents and opponents [48,55,56,114,116–118,120,121,130,131,145]. Researchers described and discussed these practices in more depth through accounts of other actors and their experiences with these practices. Often, these analyses “through the eyes of others” discuss practices that are characterized as having harmful consequences for local communities and opponents. Though se

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ces for local communities and opponents. Though several of these publications were out of scope for this literature review, we do feel it is important to share this observation, as it illus- trates the aforementioned “obsession” of social science researchers with the public [28] and perhaps also hints at the tendencies to vilify RET actors and romanticize opponents [19]. The above observations poi

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: nticize opponents [19]. The above observations point to two ‘tendencies’ of researchers investigating RET actors: presenting RET actor research as biased and investigating RET actors through the perspective of other stakeholders. These tendencies could be born out of necessity and pragmatism: several researchers [43,56,130] report that developers were not willing to participate in research project

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ere not willing to participate in research projects due to existing tensions in controversies, or were only willing to respond via a written statement. Getting access to RET actors is thus an important challenge for social science researchers. The two tendencies could also indicate potential implicit assump- tions among researchers regarding which perspectives, and subse- quently which information

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: perspectives, and subse- quently which information, is considered relevant and legitimate when investigating energy controversies. This is potentially problematic as such research often serves as a starting point for recommendations to RET actors: what they should and should not do from the perspective of the affected or opposing stakeholders, or according to the researchers (for example [48,63,97

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ccording to the researchers (for example [48,63,97,145]). As a research field that prides itself on a critical and reflective attitude, it might be insightful to apply this atti- tude to our own assumptions regarding RET actors. One could argue that RET actors are generally in a more powerful position compared to communities and those opposing projects, and therefore the latter are often provided

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: ects, and therefore the latter are often provided with a voice by researchers as a type of counterhegemonic action. Such an approach is justifiable as a way of addressing power asymmetries. At the same time, the importance of investigating powerful actors and elites has long been pointed out [146,147]. By “obsessing” over publics [28], we run the risk of missing or misinterpreting potential valuab

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: isk of missing or misinterpreting potential valuable insights when investigating renewable energy controversies. We may very well come to different conclusions or recommendations if we opened up our research attention 9 E. van de Grift and E. Cuppen to include RET actors – especially given our finding that RET actors are treated with low resolution, i.e., as a rather homogeneous group, without muc

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: , i.e., as a rather homogeneous group, without much attention to diversity among RET actors. As a field, we need to have discussions on how to investigate multiple actors and perspectives, without compromising our ability to address apparent power asymmetries. 5. Conclusion With this literature review we set out to map the current state of knowledge on RET actors in controversies surrounding renew

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: e on RET actors in controversies surrounding renewable en- ergy technologies and infrastructure. Reviewing the existing empirical social science research on RET actors reveals two main categories in this body of literature: RET actors' perceptions of public opposition (Section 3.1) and their responses to public opposition (Section 3.2). Reflecting on the literature, we made three observations (Sec

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: on the literature, we made three observations (Section 4): (1) The diversity of RET actors, types of energy technologies or geographical areas is not reflected in the body of literature; (2) Publications are mainly of a descriptive character; (3) there is little research on how RET actors (attempt to) accommodate public concerns. Reflecting on the reviewed literature in the context of the broader

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: reviewed literature in the context of the broader field reveals that research that does focus on RET actors is on multiple occasions presented as biased. In addition, it often investigates RET actors through the eyes of others, emphasizing a counterhegemonic perspective on renewable energy controversies. With this review, we add to the current literature by providing a substantiation for existing

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: rature by providing a substantiation for existing calls [19,42,44,45] to broaden the scope of social science research. In the discussion (Section 4), we have provided a number of suggestions for future research. These suggestions translate into an agenda for future social science research into RET actors involved in energy controversies: 1. The reviewed literature (with a few exceptions) currently

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: iewed literature (with a few exceptions) currently shows a one-dimensional, generic picture of RET actors, their perspectives and practices. As such, this diversity of RET actors and their practices in the context of controversies needs to be unpacked. Future research also needs to diversify in terms of renewable energy technologies and geographical contexts. This also opens up opportunities for c

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: l contexts. This also opens up opportunities for comparative research. 2. Due to the mainly descriptive character research into RET actors, future research needs to be aimed at providing explanations for RET actors' perspectives and practices. Such explanatory research helps build a more fine-grained and rich understanding of RET actors that goes beyond current generic descriptions. Besides these

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: eyond current generic descriptions. Besides these two general directions, we want to make two addi- tional points resulting from our research design. First, as mentioned earlier, our methodological choice to only include publications in En- glish means we are potentially missing valuable insights into RET actors Appendix A. Flow chart of selection publications for systematic lite 1 E n e r g y R e

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: publications for systematic lite 1 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 91(2022)102749 in the context of controversies published in other languages. As such, future research should include publications in other languages. Second, as we reviewed RET actors in the context of societal conflict means that related studies on this group which do not explicitly focus on societal confli

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: p which do not explicitly focus on societal conflict were not included. Such publications could provide valuable insights in terms of practices regarding engagement with (local) publics and communities (for example [134,148]). Reviewing RET actors, their perspectives and practices without societal conflict as a demarcation would provide a more comprehensive picture. There is a clear need to furthe

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: rehensive picture. There is a clear need to further unpack the metaphorical black box of RET actors. Such research can contribute to a more fine-grained un- derstanding of the (development of) conflict dynamics between the ac- tors engaged in controversies and action perspectives for the involved actors. These insights would allow researchers to identify challenges and opportunities in the governa

authors: van de Grift, E., Cuppen, E.
title: Beyond the public in controversies: A systematic review on social opposition and renewable energy actors
text: entify challenges and opportunities in the governance of renewable energy controversies, and provide recommendations for just and democratic energy transitions that are more attuned to RET actors. This would be both timely and necessary considering the continuation and upscaling of energy transi- tions worldwide. Summerfield-Ryan articulates the concept of embodied political ecology and situates it within the literature on global supply chains and solar PV specifically. Section two details the methodology for the study. Section three then examines the global market for

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: Section three then examines the global market for solar PV highlighting dependence on China and Chinese companies. Section four then maps the socio- ecological harms throughout the life cycle onto the global supply chain to document the embodied political ecology of the commodity. Section five sketches attempts to govern solar PV supply chains. The final section concludes with areas for further r

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: e final section concludes with areas for further research. 2. Embodied political ecology Facilitated by decades of globalization, an estimated 70 % of inter- national trade takes place through global supply chains [11]. These are functionally and geographically fragmented networks led by lead firms that seek to find the greatest economic advantage in having parts of commodities produced in stages

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: in having parts of commodities produced in stages using different suppliers, producers, and factories [c.f. [12]. Global supply chains have significant socio- ecological impacts [7,13,14], with analysis frequently focusing on spe- cific issues, corporations, and sectors to ascertain whether companies are meeting environmental, social, and governance standards. Many environmental and social standar

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: e standards. Many environmental and social standards are established by multinational corporations pre-empting regulation or to demonstrate their corporate social responsibility considering reputational risks from controversies informing shareholder and consumer behaviour [15]. A range of voluntary transnational governance initiatives for RE now exist with many driven by companies, states, and int

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ist with many driven by companies, states, and international organi- zations including the EU and the OECD, as well as non-government or- ganizations in a variety of multistakeholder initiatives [10,16]. Private or non-state market governance [17,18], includes an abundance of voluntary codes, self-defined standards, certification systems, and public reporting [19,20]. LeBaron and Lister [8] review

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: c reporting [19,20]. LeBaron and Lister [8] reviewed 290 studies of the effectiveness of initiatives to govern global supply chains and observed that they often do not meet the criteria for minimising impacts even against their own metrics. This reinforces concerns that global supply chain governance lacks accountability given they are designed by corporate actors whose primary interest is not mit

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: corporate actors whose primary interest is not mitigating socio- ecological impacts [7,9]. Rather than focusing on specific impacts, this paper argues that commodities like solar PV have an ‘embodied political ecology’ or a range of socio-ecological impacts that occur throughout the product life cycle and therefore along the global supply chain. This is a conceptually driven argument that builds o

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: is is a conceptually driven argument that builds on concepts from the multidisciplinary literature on energy and supply chains such as ‘embodied energy injustice’ [21–23] and ‘shadow ecology’ [24] to recognise a plethora of socio-ecological impacts that arise across temporal and spatial scales from producing these commodities for global markets. The term ‘embodied’ captures the idea from the build

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: e term ‘embodied’ captures the idea from the building industry that energy and emissions created for construction should be counted in its impact, which scholars have argued should also include environmental in- justices as detailed below [21]. A solar panel, like a building, has embodied emissions as well as contributing to socio-ecological impacts such as extractivist violence, eco-toxicity, pol

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: s such as extractivist violence, eco-toxicity, pollution, water depletion, community impacts, and forced labour, as documented below. We invoke the concept of ‘political ecology’ to capture these harms, where political ecology is an interdisciplinary field that recognises that environmental change is embedded in social, economic, and political interactions [25]. It investigates how economic struct

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ractions [25]. It investigates how economic structures and power relations impact socio-ecological relations across temporal and spatial scales [26]. Previously [24] used the concept of ‘shadow ecology’ to demonstrate how the international trade of tropical timber to Japan cast an ecological shadow over South-East Asian forests. ‘Embodied political ecology’ thus captures the inherent socio-ecologi

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ecology’ thus captures the inherent socio-ecological impacts of political and economic decision making throughout the life cycle of producing commodities like solar PV for a global market. Linking ‘embodied’ to ‘political ecology’ seeks to recognise the political and economic forces 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 1 27 (2025) 104212 that shape the socio-ecological impacts

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 25) 104212 that shape the socio-ecological impacts of solar PV throughout its life cycle and therefore the global supply chain. Other concepts identify negative impacts but tend not to capture their embodiment in the commodity. For example, scholars invoke ‘sacrifice zones’ to explain economic and political decisions to contain environmental impacts and send waste elsewhere [27]. The environ- ment

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: s and send waste elsewhere [27]. The environ- mental justice movement used sacrifice zones to draw attention to the siting of extraction and polluting industries in primarily poor and people of colour communities, such that they had to bear the unjust burden of toxic pollution, waste, and chemical exposure [28]. However, sacrifice zones are static because scholars have examined the impact in a par

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: because scholars have examined the impact in a particular zone rather than embodied throughout global supply chains. Recently, Brock et al. [29] argued that energy is embodied and multi- scalar because new sacrifice zones may open along global solar PV chains, although they examine only one site. Others have identified three types of global displacement from global RE: dispossession, which displac

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ement from global RE: dispossession, which displaces people and communities from land and livelihood through land grabs and resource exploitation; the pollution and degradation of ecosystems from the local to the global throughout the global supply chain; and resource dependence, or sys- tematic patterns of unequal exchange that lock states into “destructive development dependencies of primary ext

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: estructive development dependencies of primary extraction, land expropriation, elite capture, and unsafe disposal of toxic and hazardous waste” [1]. To date, this has only been applied to the extraction point. More frequently used is the concept of embodied energy injustice, which seeks to encapsulate the injustices that occur to people along the supply chain. This shows that injustices to communi

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: upply chain. This shows that injustices to communities including environmental degradation often fall outside environmental impact as- sessments, occur across different jurisdictions and timescales, are disconnected from the sites of combustion and consumption, and are “hidden or removed from sites of political power” [21, p. 220]. Although they overlook environmental impact assessments undertaken

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: erlook environmental impact assessments undertaken at the extraction and production points, the argument is that the entirety of the global supply chain is not captured in the assessment of the commod- ities' impacts. These are defined as “transboundary socio-environmental injustices linked to energy policy decision-making” and include both quantitative measurable impacts such as emissions and qua

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ative measurable impacts such as emissions and qualitative im- pacts such as forcible displacement of people [21, pp. 220, 226]. They identify distributional, procedural, and recognition energy injustices with an inductive list of impacts detailed further below (see Table 3). In relation to solar PV, Mulvaney [22] argues that analysis of embodied energy injustice should identify the unit of analys

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: nergy injustice should identify the unit of analysis, from the solar park to the panel or solar cell. Recognizing supply chain complexity means selecting the material to follow through the chain. Here we seek to connect the socio-ecological impacts to the LCA cate- gories of extraction, processing, and manufacturing; installation; oper- ation and maintenance; and end-of-life of solar PV to the sup

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: aintenance; and end-of-life of solar PV to the supply chain to ascertain how best to minimise them. Alternatively, Sovacool et al. [23] argue for a whole systems approach that includes multiple places and scales. This captures injustices from the use of smart grids to electric vehicles and solar panels. Using three scales from the micro- (commu- nity) level, meso- (national) level, and macro- (glo

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ty) level, meso- (national) level, and macro- (global) level, the research inductively identifies 44 injustices stemming from the energy transition. This enables the identification of four types of injustices: demographic; spatial; interspecies; and temporal [23]. The variety of injustices across the life cycle of RE technologies can be applied to different scales. We add socio-ecological impacts

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: different scales. We add socio-ecological impacts from the LCA literature to the harms identified inductively in this literature. The concept of embodied polit- ical ecology recognises the power dynamics inherent in energy decision making for producing commodities for global markets at each point in the global supply chain, without necessarily situating impacts in justice frameworks that stem from

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ating impacts in justice frameworks that stem from Western political philosophy [30]. We particularly wanted to document all of the ecological impacts arising from the extraction, production, use and disposal of solar panels to demonstrate the cumulative impact of their rise. Renewable energy 2 S. Park and O. Summerfield-Ryan uptake is positive for addressing decarbonisation but there are myriad s

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: addressing decarbonisation but there are myriad socio-ecological impacts throughout their supply chains and lifecycles. These impacts should be assessed along the global supply chain [31], particularly because RE generation is often dependent on conventional energy, thus increasing its socio-ecological impacts [32]. More radical scholars argue that global capitalist forces are contributing to an

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: t global capitalist forces are contributing to an ecocide-genocide nexus [33]. This argument is that energy projects are often located in areas of Indigenous peoples and contribute to the further destruction of the planet through unsustainable production and consumption. The thesis highlights how capitalism contributes to global environmental change and the manifold injustices that stem from energ

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: e and the manifold injustices that stem from energy decision-making around the world. As York and Bell [34] observe, energy ‘transitions’ have historically involved an in- crease (in absolute terms) of the fuel source supposedly being transi- tioned away from, notwithstanding a decrease in relative terms. The necessity of decarbonisation means our current sustainability transition must see a phase

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: current sustainability transition must see a phase out of fossil fuel use on the one hand, and a rapid in- crease in the uptake of renewable energy technologies on the other. We recognise the importance of going beyond an energy ‘transition’ which is primarily viewed as a change in fuel sources or a ‘technological fix’. Rather, there are underlying processes such as a continued commitment to econo

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: processes such as a continued commitment to economic growth that also must be re-evaluated as part of a sus- tainability ‘transformation’ (for example, see [35]). In the following section we outline the methodology for identifying and tracking the socio-ecological impacts of solar PV through LCA and non-LCA studies before tracing them along the global supply chain. 3. Methodology We articulate th

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: obal supply chain. 3. Methodology We articulate the embodied political ecology of solar PV via two ways: through the LCA approach which is often used in geo-engineering to capture primarily ecological, energy, and health harms, and through non-LCA studies that capture social impacts derived from a compre- hensive literature review, including the RE transition literature dis- cussed above. LCA stud

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: transition literature dis- cussed above. LCA studies use a standardised assessment framework to document impacts from energy systems (ISO 1440-2006). LCAs commonly identify four lifecycle stages: 1) raw material extraction, processing, and manufacturing; 2) installation; 3) operation and main- tenance (O&M); and 4) end-of-life (EoL) including decommissioning and disposal. As illustrated in Table

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: mmissioning and disposal. As illustrated in Table 1which summarises our literature re- view findings, LCA studies have often focused on energy-related envi- ronmental indicators (e.g. Energy Pay Back Time [EPBT], and GHG emissions) [36,37]. This is important because it brings together a comprehensive list of all of the known impacts from producing solar PV using this approach, and provides a basel

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: solar PV using this approach, and provides a baseline for identifying ecological and human health impacts of solar PV. This is detailed below. Although LCAs are useful tools to identify impacts throughout the lifecycle of a given technology, certain socio-ecological harms are difficult to quantify and are more appropriately documented using qualitative methods. For example, LCA's focus on energy-r

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: tive methods. For example, LCA's focus on energy-related metrics obscure impacts such as social dispossession and poor labor practices, including forced labor. Additionally, as DuPuis and Mulvaney [78] and Silvast et al. [79] argue, the use of models often conceals underlying assumptions and values which frame how problems are represented and addressed. Mulvaney [80] observes these methodological

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ssed. Mulvaney [80] observes these methodological limitations in relation to solar PV. We therefore supplement the LCA literature with non-LCA studies to assess a broader range of impacts across the lifecycle of solar PV using three databases: ScienceDirect, Web of Science Core Collection, and Scopus. We used the following search terms: “Environmental Impact” AND (“Solar” OR “Photovoltaic”); (“Tox

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: tal Impact” AND (“Solar” OR “Photovoltaic”); (“Toxicity” OR “Eco-Toxicity”) AND (“Solar” OR “Photovoltaic”); “Land Use” AND (“Solar” OR “Photovol- taic”); “Water Use” AND (“Solar” OR “Photovoltaic”); “Life Cycle” AND (“Solar” OR “Photovoltaic”); (“Biodiversity” OR “Ecosystem”) AND (“Solar” OR “Photovoltaic”). Google scholar was added to ensure that we did not miss work from 2010 on using the searc

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: we did not miss work from 2010 on using the search terms “solar” and 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 1 27 (2025) 104212 “LCA” and “solar” and “end-of-life.” We attempt to capture the full spectrum of socio-ecological impacts resulting from solar PV from cradle to grave in the concept of embodied political ecology. The following table identifies the known inductive impacts

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: owing table identifies the known inductive impacts derived from the literature on global energy and solar supply chains using the following search terms “energy injustice” and “solar energy injustice” and “climate justice” and “social impacts” and “solar supply chains.” One of the limitations of the method using these keywords was that it did not adequately capture significant literatures in polit

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: dequately capture significant literatures in political ecology, including from black [81] and feminist scholarship [82–84], and the extensive literature on extractivist violence from mining.1While many of the impacts are captured (see Table 3), including uneven distribution of project costs and benefits particularly by race, gender, and caste; dispossession; and land use conflicts; our approach ma

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ossession; and land use conflicts; our approach may seem ‘disem- bodied’ from the site of violence particularly at the point of extraction of critical minerals mining for solar PV. This is an unfortunate trade off in attempting to identify and trace all known harms along the life cycle of the commodity to the global supply chain. Drawing together the full impacts of solar PV into the concept of em

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: he full impacts of solar PV into the concept of embodied political ecology builds on current concepts that are limited in how they apply to global supply chains, and the site specific and inductive approach they take to investigating their impacts. Embodied political ecology provides a way to capture all known socio-ecological impacts from all political and economic decisions along the supply chai

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: tical and economic decisions along the supply chain, thus incorporating findings of site and stage specific studies into a broader conceptualization of the commodity. This helps to locate where and by whom such political and economic decisions are made, thus providing the basis for interventions to improve supply chain gover- nance. We hope the concept of embodied political ecology contributes to

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: cept of embodied political ecology contributes to building connections between grounded fieldwork and global value chain research. The benefit of such a concept is that it can be applied to all (RE) supply chains, with more work to be done identifying how and why certain decisions that lead to these outcomes are made. Next the global solar PV supply chain is documented before mapping its impacts a

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: y chain is documented before mapping its impacts across each stage, which reveals the need for greater transparency by China and Chinese companies, and more stringently governed global solar PV supply chains. 3.1. Solar PV's global supply chain By 2023, cumulative installed capacity for solar PV reached between 1581 and 1624 GW ( [114]; on comparing RE see Fig. 1). The bulk of RE investment has be

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: g RE see Fig. 1). The bulk of RE investment has been for solar PV and wind [115]. Globally the cost of utility-scale solar PV declined 85 % between 2010 and 2020; RE is now cost-competitive with fossil fuels in many parts of the world [115–117]. This illustrates the importance of solar PV for the global energy transi- tion. This section outlines the market and geographic concentration of solar PV

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: e market and geographic concentration of solar PV supply chain to locate where these processes are taking place and therefore where the socio-ecological impacts occur. As shown in Table 4, China now accounts for most of the global installed capacity with a minimum additional 235.5 GW installed at the end of 2023. As demonstrated below, a similar trend is evident in solar PV production and manufact

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: end is evident in solar PV production and manufacturing. Over the past decade, solar PV manufacturing has increasingly shifted to China from Europe, the US, and Japan [119]. This reflects China's more than US$50 billion investment in additional supply ca- pacity since 2011 and its focus on the PV industry as a strategically important sector [120–123]. This has created significant geographical conc

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 3]. This has created significant geographical concentration (see Fig. 2), with many states relying on imported solar PV modules. Between 2017 and 2021 the EU imported 84 % of its solar PV modules; the US 77 % [119]. There are five key stages in the solar PV manufacturing process, each 1 We thank the reviewers for pointing this out. 3 S. Park and O. Summerfield-Ryan Table 1 Socio-ecological Impacts

In 2021, there were 38 states with capacity for module assembly, although many of these were small or not fully developed [119,p. 26]. Despite this, China still accounts for 77.8 % of module production [119,125]. China is also the leading producer of ma

text: 119,125]. China is also the leading producer of many of the critical minerals used in solar PV [126–129]. As a result, China, and a small number of Chinese companies, increasingly dominate each component of solar PV manufacturing (see Table 5). China's dominance highlights several issues. Moreover, one out of every seven panels pro- duced worldwide is manufacturing by a single facility. This level of concentration in any global supply chain would represent a considerable vulnerability (p. 9). Second, solar PV deployment may be slowed by an increasing number of

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: eployment may be slowed by an increasing number of trade disputes between China, the US and the EU [130–132], supply chain bottlenecks, and financial volatility in the sector which has led to several bankruptcies [119]. Indeed, the escalation of present trade disputes between the United States and China (among others) demon- strates the vulnerability of global supply chains more broadly to growing

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ty of global supply chains more broadly to growing economic nationalism. Third, the supply chains for critical minerals required for RE and battery storage are also concentrated and reliant on China, which are a cause of geopolitical tension (discussed below). A preferable option, they argue, is to redesign PV panels and substitute or minimise the use of toxic materials, as well as “incorporating social, economic and environmental benefits to achieve up-cycling with all the materials” [151,p. 56]. Increasing the life span of PV panels/modules will also reduce material requireme

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: panels/modules will also reduce material requirements [54,73]. In sum, while many of the minerals for solar PV are extracted around the world, processing of minerals and manufacturing of the various component parts of solar PV largely takes place in China and by Chinese companies. This means that many of the decisions that have the biggest socio-ecological impacts are taken by China and Chinese co

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ological impacts are taken by China and Chinese companies. This section has highlighted the

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: global problem. This section has highlighted the various stages of the solar PV life cycle to demonstrate why and where the socio-ecological impacts are occurring. This better identifies how energy decisions along the life cycle shape the embodied political ecology of producing solar PV for a global market. The following section examines efforts to govern the global solar PV supply chain to minim

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: o govern the global solar PV supply chain to minimise its embodied political ecology. 3.3. Minimising the embodied political ecology in global solar PV chains As noted from the outset, voluntary non-state market governance is used to mitigate the socio-ecological impacts of global commodities, although it is generally ineffective in doing so. Governance of solar PV ranges from technical and procur

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: nance of solar PV ranges from technical and procurement standards, ecolabels, and in- dustry association standards to more stringent requirements including US legislation to stop solar PV made by forced labour at the border, and an EU Directive on waste. There are therefore a variety of ways in which the embodied political ecology of solar PV is being addressed.7However, harder regulations for glo

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ing addressed.7However, harder regulations for global supply chains to mitigate socio-ecological impacts remain rare [7,9,10] and may face greater difficulty in being established given tensions between the US and China, particularly around solar PV. This section shows what is being done to address specific impacts from solar PV global supply chains, which only skims the surface of the many decisio

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: , which only skims the surface of the many decisions that are made in producing solar PV for a global market that contribute to harm. Indeed, as the previous section shows, at each stage of the life cycle there are options for producing a commodity that have fewer negative environmental and social impacts. The International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) is a hybrid in- ternational organization

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ion (IEC) is a hybrid in- ternational organization comprised of national committees of public officials and private representatives. It establishes standards and certi- fication for infrastructure and the international trade of electrical and electronic goods. It has developed guidelines for meeting standards and certification for the performance of solar PV, including IEC 61215 for c- Si PV. Whil

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: f solar PV, including IEC 61215 for c- Si PV. While manufacturing will remain in China for the near future, France and South Korea have adopted standards and procurement pro- cesses to reduce the emissions intensity of solar PV manufacturing. In the United States, a ‘voluntary procurement standard’ was anticipated for 2023, with benchmarks for GHG emissions to meet ‘low carbon’ and ‘ultra-low carb

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: emissions to meet ‘low carbon’ and ‘ultra-low carbon’ certifications for PV modules. This is being under- taken by the Global Electronics Council, which is a US non-government organization. Its EPEAT registry provides third-party verification and ecolabelling for solar PV products throughout their life cycle including module recycling and Energy Star manufacturing equipment [104,161]. In the US, t

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ar manufacturing equipment [104,161]. In the US, the Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA) has looked 7 The United Nations is trying to establish traceability protocols for critical minerals to mitigate socio-ecological impacts [160]. 10 S. Park and O. Summerfield-Ryan to address forced labour concerns by recommending divestment from Xinjiang and developing traceability protocols in the suppl

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: and developing traceability protocols in the supply chain. SEIA has also received pledges from approximately 250 manufacturers to increase transparency in their supply chains and vulnerabilities to forced labour [104,105]. In 2023, the SEIA became an ‘accredited standards development organisation,’ following approval from the American Na- tional Standards Institute. In September 2024, the SEIA [16

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: andards Institute. In September 2024, the SEIA [162] released its Solar and Energy Storage Supply Chain Traceability Standard 101 for public comment. Alternatively, the Solar Stewardship Initiative, that emerged out of Europe, is a multistakeholder initiative comprised of solar manufacturers and buyers, civil society, and trade associations. It has 40 members and is a “solar-specific sustainabilit

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: 40 members and is a “solar-specific sustainability assurance scheme based on independent third-party verification” [163], In late 2023 they created an ESG standard for the solar industry and have just released a traceability standard [163]. Stringent rules have been applied to solar PV commodities. When the forced labour allegations came to light the US announced an executive action in June 2021

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: the US announced an executive action in June 2021 for US Customs and Border Protection to stop silica- based materials entering at the border if they could not provide evidence of their origin. Shortly after, the US passed the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (the UFLP Act) which became law in December 2021 [133]. The Act, which came into effect from 21 June 2022, has banned goods produced in Xi

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: from 21 June 2022, has banned goods produced in Xinjiang [125] (see below). China has in turn responded to these changes by shifting polysilicon production to Inner Mongolia [125]. US efforts may shift towards ‘reshoring’ (increasing domestic production capacity of certain parts of the supply chain) and shifting away from c-Si PV technologies towards thin-film and third- generation PV technologies

title: The embodied political ecology of solar energy: Tracing socio-ecological impacts through solar photovoltaic global supply chains
text: ds thin-film and third- generation PV technologies to avoid the many of the socio-ecological impacts of the current global supply chain [104,105]. Greater trans- parency in Chinese governance of the solar industry is needed to un- derstand how negative socio-ecological impacts are being managed and mitigated. While the US has led efforts to tackle forced labour in the global solar PV supply chain,

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: of mouth, which drives potential adopters to accept or reject an innovation [72,73]. This paper aims to address these research gaps by placing the views of current end users of renewable energy microgrids that live in NIZ in low-income commu- nities in a developing country first and comparing it to people in their neighbouring communities without such systems. This is especially important since th

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: uch systems. This is especially important since the social dimensions of diffusing microgrids often determine whether an innovation is successfully adopted by the poten- tial beneficiaries [9]. The Wayuu people in La Guajira, Colombia, were chosen as a case for this study since they reside in a NIZ zone where decentralised solar energy microgrids have started to be deployed on a small scale [25,26

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ave started to be deployed on a small scale [25,26]. At the same time, the vast majority of the region’s rural population still suffers from energy poverty [25]. The study aims to identify the specific enablers and barriers to microgrid diffusion and how the presence of microgrids amongst neighbours impacts the willingness to adopt them by non-users. The rest of this paper is structured as follows

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: s. The rest of this paper is structured as follows: 1. The article examines Colombia’s main rural electrification chal- lenges and legislations and presents the case study used in this research. 2. Presents the theoretical framework and factors determining the successful diffusion of microgrids. 3. Describes the research methodology and the characteristics of the study communities. 4. Analyses and

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: eristics of the study communities. 4. Analyses and discuss decentralised renewable energy microgrid diffusion in the study context and its implications for Colombia’s last-mile electrification goals. 5. Concludes the article. 1.1. The Colombian context In Colombia, one of the main electrification challenges is the rural energy poor’s geographical isolation, making connecting them to the national e

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: solation, making connecting them to the national electricity grid inherently hard [1]. The difficulty hindering providing the rural energy poor with clean and reliable energy is that 52 % of Colombian territory lies in NIZ, making it difficult and expensive to connect them to the national grid [8]. The NIZ are areas which do not have access to the National Interconnected System of energy (SIN), as

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: National Interconnected System of energy (SIN), as seen in Fig. 1 and have an estimated population of 1,900,000 inhabitants [67], with a population density of three inhabitants per square kilometre [66]. Comparably, the average population density in Colombia as of 2020 is 46 people per square kilometre of land area [68]. This low population density and geographical isolation make it technically an

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: and geographical isolation make it technically and economically non-viable to connect these NIZ areas to the SIN [1]. Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 2, the density of the NIZ varies. Some parts of the NIZ have a much lower population density than three inhabitants per square kilometre, making it even harder to connect these areas to the SIN [66]. The people with energy in Colombia’s rural areas typ

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: e people with energy in Colombia’s rural areas typically depend on diesel gen-sets that face an insecure supply [71]. Currently, 95 % of people with energy access in Colombia’s NIZ use diesel, and the remaining 5 % use renewable energy, driven by the high subsidies provided by the national government to fuel suppliers, reaching more than USD 33 million in 2018 [1]. The Colombian government aims to

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: lion in 2018 [1]. The Colombian government aims to address this and has, since 2015, created an enabling environment for non-conventional renewable energy technologies [1]. Colombia has two relevant legislations supporting the diffusion of renewable energy technologies in NIZ – Article 185 of the National Development Plan (2014–2018) (Law 1753 (2015)) [27] and Law 1715 (2014) [28]. title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: prior frameworks and theories guide the study design, data collection and analysis [50]. Three different research methods are used to underpin the comparative approach, namely ob- servatory research, semi-structured interviews, and focus group dis- cussions (FGDs). In total, 33 Wayuus from five distinct communities took part in this study – 17 participants in the semi-structured interviews and 16

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ticipants in the semi-structured interviews and 16 different participants in the FGDs, as seen in Appendix A. Since semi-structured interviews allows for questions to be amended during the interview process and helps create and direct a fluid con- versation while keeping the participants’ focus on the research topic it was chosen as the primary research method [53]. Furthermore, espe- cially impor

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: earch method [53]. Furthermore, espe- cially important when working with communities with different access to energy, semi-structured interviews allow for possible varied views and knowledge amongst the participants to be included [51]. The in- terviews lasted approximately 20 to 50 min. Twelve were conducted in- person and five were conducted via phone. Seven men and 10 women participated in the

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: phone. Seven men and 10 women participated in the interviews, as seen in Appendix A. All respondents were asked the same questions; however, relevant follow-up questions and elaborations by the participants were included for clarification purposes when needed [51]. Six questions about the participants char- acteristics were asked in the beginning to start the interview. A total of 12 to 23 questio

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: o start the interview. A total of 12 to 23 questions, including follow-up questions, were asked relating the reliability of the systems in providing energy, usage, relationships with other people using energy, how the system was acquired, cultural aspects, opinions, benefits, and the system’s relative advantage. The FGDs were chosen as an additional research method since it allows for more of an i

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: l research method since it allows for more of an informal discussion to take place by departing from topics and discussion questions similar to the ones used during the semi-structured interviews [49]. Three FGDs, each with distinctive participants, took place in the Manaure municipality in La Guajira. The participants in the first FGD, including one man and three women, used a solar driven microgrid system

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: three women, used a solar driven microgrid system that pumps groundwater. Two women and five men participated in the second FGD. Four of them lacked access to any energy and three had access to a solar microgrid that pumps groundwater. title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: as seen in Fig. 7. The last research method deployed in this study is observational research. The researcher was shown five sites.
