The Wayuu people’s strong community ties were directly observed when visiting this site with numerous

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ly observed when visiting this site with numerous individuals from different neighbouring households visiting to collect drinking water. The last site was used by people completely lacking energy driven groundwater pumping systems and consisted of a well where users had to manually throw down a bucket and pull up the water. Water was thereafter collected in tanks of roughly five to thirty litres.

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: lected in tanks of roughly five to thirty litres. This water was transported either by humans or by donkey to their respective households, sometimes up to 90 min away. All the solar microgrids and windmill systems in this study are used for groundwater abstraction, generally for agriculture, live- stock production, and domestic purposes. This study’s ethical procedures followed the guidelines set out by Lund University [51]. Consent forms were created together with the translator and explained to the participants in their language. Ver

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: plained to the participants in their language. Verbal consent was asked due to a lack of reading and writing capabilities amongst the respondents. Results and discussion The individuals representing the group with solar microgrids expressed strong relative advantages since they had acquired access to the innovation. Prior to the solar microgrids, they used to access water through either the older windmill systems or manually by throwing a bucket down a well and pulling it up. All solar energy users stated that the

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ing it up. All solar energy users stated that the new systems had increased their water access which subsequently had led to high water security for the majority as seen in Fig. 9. As explained by one participant: “previously when there was no solar energy we had a lot of work, but now that we have it, we have benefited from it, and we have not suffered so much from the water problem.” Another ela

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: fered so much from the water problem.” Another elaborated on how it helps fulfil several of their needs when comparing it to pre- vious ways of accessing water by stating that “it is a good technology, and I am happy that I can use it for water supply, for food, for agriculture, for livestock and animals.” However, although all respondents using solar microgrids reported strong relative advantages

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: lar microgrids reported strong relative advantages, three solar energy re- spondents stated that the new systems had only been able to give them a moderate level of water security since the water provided by the systems they used was not enough to use for agriculture. These three respondents had accessed water from the same well through a windmill groundwater pumping system prior to the diffusion

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: groundwater pumping system prior to the diffusion of the solar microgrid. They all expressed worries about the increasing lack of water but did not attri- bute it to the solar microgrid, instead, that they had used the same well for too long. Despite this difficulty, they all reported that the solar microgrid functioned better than their previous windmill system. Therefore, the ability to increase

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: indmill system. I just wish I had it, if you have more money, you want to buy one of those systems.” All par- ticipants mentioned similar views about the cost of solar microgrids, and the ones without such systems all stated that the little money they ha

text: h systems all stated that the little money they had was needed to buy food to survive and that saving up to buy a solar microgrid was not a possibility. This was further reflected by the people with solar or windmill systems, who had received their systems as do- nations from NGOs, IGOs, an MNC operating in the area, or the local government. They all expressed that if it were not for the donation,

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ll expressed that if it were not for the donation, they would not be able to have a microgrid. During one of the FGDs, only one participant mentioned that they had bought a small solar microgrid on a lease agreement; however, it had to be returned due to its relatively high cost. Aligning with the literature on diffusing innovations in low-income communities in developing countries [13,25,26], the

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ommunities in developing countries [13,25,26], the main barrier to solar microgrid diffusion was therefore identified in this study as the lack of financial resources due to low incomes and the high initial costs of renewable energy technologies. This barrier is likely underpinned by the widespread poverty amongst the Wayuu people and high govern- mental neglect which has contributed to the Wayuus

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: mental neglect which has contributed to the Wayuus harsh living con- ditions [38,39]. Furthermore, another important factor to consider is the increasing climate change impacts facing La Guajira [65]. This has led to growing water and food insecurity which adversely impacts the Wayuu people’s livelihood opportunities such as being able to engage in agri- culture and livestock production, leaving t

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: agri- culture and livestock production, leaving them with less possibilities of engaging in productive economic activities [5,7]. Moreover, the lack of financial capital further undermined another aspect of diffusion theory, mainly an innovations trialability. Only one participant stated that they had been able to try a solar energy microgrid system on a renting agreement but had to give “that ba

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: em on a renting agreement but had to give “that back because we have to be paying”. As this quote indicates, the Wayuu people’s lack of financial resources hinders their ability to try out the system, even when the initial investment cost is reduced through rental contracts of microgrids. Therefore, most Wayuu’s are unable to try out the systems, reducing their ability to see the benefits of the s

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: educing their ability to see the benefits of the systems. This is likely to have a higher negative impact on diffusion in other parts of La Guajira, where people would not have been able to observe the impacts of microgrids amongst their neighbours. As such, the largest barrier, financial constraints, further hinder the ability of social aspects of diffusing solar microgrid innovations within Wayu

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: diffusing solar microgrid innovations within Wayuu communities to be fully realized, which is likely to be even more relevant in communities that lack neighbours with such systems. An important factor worth mentioning is the view of the Wayuu people from many outsiders. Before embarking on the field study for this research, several individuals that were familiar with La Guajira mentioned that the

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: t were familiar with La Guajira mentioned that the Wayuus usually do not want microgrids and the ones that have been diffused are not widely used. Such views will likely impact the diffusion of microgrids amongst Wayuu communities by reducing the willingness to donate microgrids to the indigenous peoples in La Guajira and reduce the incentives to subsidize potential schemes that would make the hig

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: ubsidize potential schemes that would make the high initial costs of the systems lower. Several respondents without energy access mentioned such lack of communi- cation with potential diffusers as an issue that undermined their access to renewable energy microgrids. When asked whether the respondents in the no energy group had received offers to buy or receive microgrids, only one respondent had r

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: y or receive microgrids, only one respondent had received an offer. The rest of the participants stated that they did not know where they could get a microgrid even if they would afford one. This shows that the high neglect of the Wayuu people by the government and other important bodies [38] further un- dermines their ability to access microgrids, hindering their diffusion. 1 E n e r g y R e s e

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: hindering their diffusion. 1 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e101(2023)103132 Part of this lack of communication with outsiders that could distribute or sell microgrids is the widespread lack of ICTs amongst the Wayuus. Like in many low-income communities in developing countries, ICT are not widespread, making it much more difficult to communicate with rele- vant development

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: fficult to communicate with rele- vant development actors [22]. The impact of this leads many Wayuu people to become further isolated from the outside world and with the view that the Wayuus do not want microgrids risks further undermining potential future diffusion attempts. Further undermining this communication with relevant agents that might encourage diffusion of microgrids are likely the fin

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: courage diffusion of microgrids are likely the financial aspect of the Wayuus. For the private sector to encourage diffusion by providing different microgrid schemes to potential recipients there must be a financial gain for the private enterprise [61]. Since the Wayuus low purchasing power is widely known [1], it is likely further undermining the willingness by private actors to try to reach out

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: willingness by private actors to try to reach out to relevant com- munities with offers to buy microgrids. Therefore, a combination of insufficient outside communication and a lack of ability to try out the systems, underpinned by low financial capital, serves as a combination of factors which stands in the way of widespread renewable energy microgrid diffusion amongst the Wayuu people in La Guaji

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: rid diffusion amongst the Wayuu people in La Guajira. However, as this study shows, the Wayuu’s participating in this research are highly willing to adopt renewable energy systems and use the ones in place effectively. Therefore, with relevant support, especially financial, renewable energy microgrids could be effectively diffused amongst La Guajira’s Wayuu people. 4.1. Relevance for Colombia’s in

title: What makes Colombia's indigenous peoples adopt microgrids? Social acceptance and financial constraints in renewable energy diffusion
text: a’s Wayuu people. 4.1. While each of the aforementioned literature reviews address a different set of social outcomes, their common characteristic lies with the treatment of inequalities as negative social outcomes. Put differ- ently, inequalities enter the assessment of C&LCTP in an ex-post fashion even if they are present before C&LCTP

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: st fashion even if they are present before C&LCTP are implemented or designed. Meanwhile, recent literature have increasingly shifted away from the treatment of inequalities as “unfortunate side circumstance(s) of capi- talism” ([68], p.18, (brackets added)) and emphasises that economic and social inequalities are systemic and entrenched in socio-economic and political structures [42,69,70]. This

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: conomic and political structures [42,69,70]. This paper follows this line of thought and argues that the analytical consideration of inequalities as an outcome of C&LCTP is limiting in two ways. First, it denies an assess- ment of the way in which systemic inequalities grant certain groups more or less privileged positions vis-a-vis specific policy proposals [61,71,72]. Second, it restricts policy

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: proposals [61,71,72]. Second, it restricts policy design to the prevention of increased inequalities while abstaining from efforts that challenge the existing structures that perpetuate systemic inequalities [32,73]. Notwithstanding, the literature reviews by Markkanen & Anger- Kraavi [13], García-García et al. [14] and Lamb et al. [15] offer valu- able insights regarding the transmission of C&LC

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: - able insights regarding the transmission of C&LCTP to a plurality of social outcomes. On the whole, this is done through the lens of various justice dimensions: 1) distributive justice, concerning the fair allocation of goods and resources benefits and costs, 2) procedural justice, concerning who takes what decisions and 3) recognition justice, concerning who is and who isn't valued [74–76]. In

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: oncerning who is and who isn't valued [74–76]. In the following section we consolidate these literature reviews into a conceptual meta-review that offers an analytical alternative to the treatment of inequalities as negative social outcomes. 3. text: be applicable to countries in the Global South. Section 3.2.1, which discusses transmission pathways, further elaborates on the C&LCTP addressed in the literature reviews. 3.2. Social outcome characteristics When it comes to social outcome characteristics we find that Mark- kanen & Anger-Kraavi [13] and Lamb et al. [15] evaluate different C&LCTP in terms of multi-dimensional social outcomes (e.g.

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: n terms of multi-dimensional social outcomes (e.g. poverty and livelihood impacts, energy affordability/access and gender inequality), while García-García et al. [14] limit their discussion of C&LCTP to social outcomes that are strictly related to income distribu- tion and employment outcomes. The literature review by Markkanen & Anger-Kraavi [13] is unique in that it discusses the impact of C&LCT

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: is unique in that it discusses the impact of C&LCTP on health inequality e.g. in terms of air pollution, air quality, access to affordable heating/cooling services but also mental and physical health. The explicit focus of García-García et al. [14] on employment effects discloses how these effects are related to labour mobility, the ability to move from one occupation to another, and skills. Since

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: from one occupation to another, and skills. Since this paper challenges the treatment of inequalities in the literature reviews, the right-side of Fig. 1 only shows “non-inequality” social outcomes which are not mutually exclusive: health, employment, livelihoods, energy access, and essential goods and services affordability. The alternative we propose is elaborated upon in the subsection below o

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: opose is elaborated upon in the subsection below on transmission pathways. 3.2.1. Transmission pathways The diversity in terms of C&LCTP and social outcomes results in distinct, though not incompatible, inferences regarding the transmission pathways from policy to outcome. While some of the contributions discuss transmission pathways by highlighting the factors that lead to negative social outcome

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: g the factors that lead to negative social outcomes, others highlight factors that ensure the pre- vention of negative social outcomes. Fig. 1 introduces a conceptual summary and re-interpretation of the transmission pathways identified in the literature reviews. Our re-interpretation suggests that each policy type passes through a filter of interrelated inequalities. As mentioned in Section 2, th

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: elated inequalities. As mentioned in Section 2, this is an important deviation from existing contributions. Instead of strictly referring to inequalities as negative social outcomes of C&LCTP, we argue that C&LCTP pass through an inequality filter and that the existing inequalities therein shape the nature of policy costs, policy benefits and policy compliance. The transmission from policies to so

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: y compliance. The transmission from policies to social outcomes through policy costs, benefits and compliance is borrowed from Lamb et al. [15] and we present the transmission pathways found in Mark- kanen & Anger-Kraavi [13] and García-García et al. [14] according to this classification. It is important to clarify the distinction between policy costs and policy compliance since complying to a pol

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: sts and policy compliance since complying to a policy bears a cost in and of itself. Transmission pathways associated with policy costs refer to the costs of implementing a policy while transmission pathways associated with policy compliance refer to the costs of complying with or adhering to a specific policy. Furthermore, costs and benefits are not necessarily monetary and as single policy type

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ot necessarily monetary and as single policy type can be subject to more than one transmission type. In sum, Fig. 1 frames social outcomes as the result of an “inequality determined” distribution of costs, benefits and compliance. In turn, this distribution potentially reinforces or diminishes the existing inequalities in the same filter. The following paragraphs are dedicated to a discus- sion of

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts

Policy costs refer to the costs associated with the implementation of a C&LCTP. title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts

Both of the aforementioned literature re- views highlight that increased energy costs represent a higher share in the budget of low-income households and therefore treat income inequality as a social outcome. Our conceptual meta-review reconstructs this statement by postulating that existing income and wealth in- equalities shape the impact of increased energy costs on energy afford- ability.2

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts

text: specifying the negative social outcomes associated with C&LCTP, the literature reviews also discuss policy proposals that can mitigate their materialisation. Markkanen & Anger- Kraavi [13] and Lamb et al. [15] stress the importanc

text: avi [13] and Lamb et al. [15] stress the importance of recognizing that disadvantaged groups and communities are more likely to experi- ence negative social outcomes. title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: ], p. 23). Markkanen & Anger-Kraavi [13] also highlight pro-poor approach

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: Anger-Kraavi [13] also highlight pro-poor approaches and argue that ITPs should ensure that the poorest households benefit most ([13], pp. 836–837). Along with Lamb et al. [15], they highlight that welfare gains or progressive outcomes have been achieved in cases where tax revenues were used either to reduce other taxes and/or increase transfers to low- income households (tax revenue recycling) ([

title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
text: low- income households (tax revenue recycling) ([13], p. 833/835, [15], pp. 11–12/21). title: Inequality determined social outcomes of low-carbon transition policies: A conceptual meta-review of justice impacts
